Instead, study the slides and READ THE BOOK. Shen doesn't talk much about the book at all but it is a life saver because that's what he tests you on, this was the only hard part about the class. Trust me.

He's a great, knowledgeable, professor IF you go to office hours and try to learn that way.  Class is useful if you aren't easily distracted, but otherwise just read the questions in the book before the exam and ask him questions in office hours.  Easy A if you do that.

Awful professor.  Ridiculous class.  Unfortunately, if you're in CS, you'll probably have to take him.  He does not explain things clearly, and thinks that because he understands the material, it should be easy for everyone.  I didn't get anything worthwhile out of the class.  Thankfully he curves high, because no one does well in his class.
Wonderful professor one of the best in my opinion. Hardest labs of my life though.

I actually really loved Scott's class, he is a caring and funny professor. We covered a lot of topics that didn't all relate to one another which made the class at times confusing. Also had to do a lot of partner projects. Overall enjoyed being in class and the topics we learned, just watch out for some random and unrelated questions on quizzes.

I did not like the flipped class structure, but Scott did a good job nonetheless. The labs and practicum did a good job helping me feel prepared for tests, but the sudden change in policy regarding internet usage during the final exam was a little jarring.
Strange way that he organized classes each week, but it kinda worked. Very hands of in his teaching, you have to go out of your way to learn the material.

He is a very hands off type of teacher. He provides video lectures that you have to watch on your own time and to understand the topics within the course. He really only covers homeworks you do in class. Keep in mind he is someone you can ask for help and very gives good feedback. For exams you 100% need to study on your own. Please also seek help.

I do not like the way Scott has his class set up. You watch videos, answer questions, and then answer the questions AGAIN, but in a team setting. Exams were so much more difficult than exercises, leaving you scrounging for time. He also seems very condescending, with him saying "GOOD" as he hears people complaining about the difficulty of his class

Scott does teach is classes "flipped" where you have to watch lecture videos outside of class, and he does assign homework on top of that. Then has an in class assignment. But it does actually prepare you for his test. He is really helpful in office hours.

Such a frustrating class. Pre-recorded lectures that only scratch the surface for most topics. Exams, quizzes, and assignments are 10x more difficult than exercises. Seems like a lot of things are done without consideration for student's time or ease of learning. Expect to write code with paper and pencil and oral exams.

Scott's a good professor when it comes to good experiences. Like, lecturing and joking around with the class as a whole, he did good and I would recommend him in that regard. His lectures were clear even with tough material. He did seem cold one-on-one, however, like he didn't know how to act with just one other person, which was awkward.

At first the class was really fun and easy and then the difficulty increased exponentially, and it was like the hardest class I've ever had. I really struggled towards the end and office hours were not has helpful as they needed to be. I highly suggest going to the computer science tutoring center if you're struggling.

Scott is nice and you can tell he cares about his students. However, his entire curriculum is not great, it is a LOT of homework, and the exams are weighted so heavily. The worst part is he doesn't teach in class, he has Youtube videos online for learning new code and simply reviews the homework in class. The exams are stressful and timed. Goodluck

This class started out manageable and pretty easy to understand, but suddenly ramped up. Lectures were more of a critique rather than support. Exam's felt completely different from the homework. The learning taught by the videos outside of class didn't provide much explanation on how to fit new concepts in with earlier ones.
Major respect to Scott for acknowledging bad student performance on exams and bringing it upon himself to help us fix that rather than moving on. Marks of an excellent teacher
rating_tags: Participation matters--Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework

review_text: His assignments were great, but so misleading about the tests. You get 50 minutes to write 2-4 separate programs, which is super hard for someone with no coding experience. I got straight As on all the homeworks and really understood the material, but the tests had nothing to do with anything we did in class. Dont take if youre new to coding

Lecture time is mainly for going over online lesson materials in groups followed by quizzes. If you are a traditional note taker and do not like canvas quizzes and panopto recordings, I'd recommend someone else.

Scott's isn't for the class for easy entry into the major. If you are to do well, you will have to learn the material deeply. I spent 2x the time in this class that I did for 301 (which is more credit hours). You really do live in the lab at some points. Tests are brutal but, in retrospect, fair and make up 50% of your grade. Always ask questions.

Absolutely loved the way Scott used a flipped classroom to teach. Lessons build up information quickly, but he's always available to get more help from (or his TA's). I didn't find this class insanely difficult, but it definitely depends on how well you understand the way Data Structures work. Midterm and Final were definitely the hardest things.
Honestly? If You're In A Class With Scott Good Luck!

He's very difficult but if you put in the work to do well in the class you will be able to gain the most out of it. If you are looking for an easy grade he is not the right professor, but if you want to learn and retain what's in the course he is the best. I always prefer having a useful and thorough professor than a useless one.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework

review_text: Scott has a good system for organizing notes. Otherwise, he is indifferent to the success of his students. He is purposefully misleading about exams, leaving the content of the midterm up in the air and completely lied about the content of the final. He ignores emails and he didn't seem interested in helping outside of class. Class final avg 47/90.

Scott absolutely cares about his students, and does his best to be available outside of class to answer any questions. Though his answers can be a bit cryptic at times, he certainly does make you think about the content and dive deeper into it.

Being his first time teaching 301, Scott was very receptive to questions during lecture and answered them with great detail. It was sometimes hard to follow his explanations on proofs during lecture due to his method of writing being somewhat cursory, though he put more detailed notes online that often helped to clear my confusion.
Messes up and stutters through lectures constantly. Exhaust all other options before you take a course with scott
Not sure how i passed this class, good luck if you take anything with Scott
rating_tags: Tough grader--Group projects--Graded by few things

review_text: His grading is if not the worst I have seen at Western. I had a A- for majority of the quarter and then 45% of our grade was determined from 1-2 weeks. We spent weeks working on a project and yet were not allowed to know how we did on it even though it was 20% of our grade. Final was 25% and we couldnt see how we did unless we went next quarter?

Class is awful if you are an independent learner/note taker. Lecture information is provided w/videos that you watch before each class. Class work is all though groups. Quizzes, midterm, and final all had questions that weren't directly covered through videos. Grading is confusing and inconsistent. Midterm and final were very but notes are allowed.

Heavy emphasis on peer instruction, but with little feedback and no posted solutions. All course content is posted online. Just look up his course website and grind LeetCode before taking the class. He's reasonable and friendly but isn't great at teaching, but he isn't the worst. Always do the extra credit for the programming assignments!

Scott doesn't post his policy about not giving out final project or Finals grades/feedback, so just know you will be kept in the dark regarding 45% of your grade. He's a lazy grader, only things scored by the TA were postrd before finals week. His response to ambiguous questions is we if you knew the material we'd know the answer. Avoid if possible

No point in coming to class when all the material you learn is through video/your group mates. Midterm felt disjointed from what we learned. Problems and Excersies also felt disjointed from material covered in the video lectures. TA for lab was very little help for problems and questions.

Although Scott is a very kind teacher, you would ask him questions and he expects you to know it from the videos. Going to his office hours he will walk through the problem on the whiteboard. Class is mandatory and you just discuss what you watched at home; no lecture in class, which is rough. Midterm was difficult but you get 1 page of notes.

Scotts 241 class covers quite a bit in a short span of time the projects can be really confusing but TA Josh Kovac was amazing and the best TA I've had at western. The in class time you cover the work you did outside of class watching lectures so if you work well in groups class is good. The mid term was the hardest part be prepared to study

Taught us via video lectures. Seems nice enough in class but is a more intimidating one on one. Projects are mostly graphics based, and all material is graded by TA's who grade on the tougher side, it also takes them weeks to get grades in. Had us take a final but proceeded not to grade it or include it in our finalized grade.

Professor Wehrwein's class was a bit difficult, but nothing too crazy. The hw is time consuming, and exams are worth about a third of your grade. As long as you do well on labs, and assignments you'll have a great chance passing his class. Remember to take good notes about important terms, and practice writing code as much as you can.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Clear grading criteria--Lots of homework

review_text: He seems like a nice person but as a teacher I found it very difficult because of him and helpers in the class being accessible and helpful throughout the class. He changed his syllabus more than halfway through the class which was frustrating, but I liked the flipped lectures, he changed our groups halfway through the quarter though.

He's a pretty quirky teacher. His lectures are "flipped," as in you watch a recorded lecture and do a set of exercises for homework and then show up for class and go over your answers with your team, and then he spends the last 15 minutes or so going over any hard problems. Annoyingly, he would never finish going over them before class ended.

Scott is the most unorganized professor I've ever had. He is inaccessible, unhelpful and completely condescending to his students. Aside from his power trip, the class is a complete mess, with the labs being the only remotely fun thing of the class, and even those are a mess. He refused to show his feedback on our final and final project. Nope.

Scott is one of the most disorganized professors I have ever met. Refused to release grading on final and final project, is not easily accessible if not in his office hours, is blatantly unhelpful, and at times extremely condescending. The content is super rushed in some parts of the class, quizzes are straight up unfair as well as the final exam.

Scott generally is approachable. Assignments took forever to be graded and most of the work wasn't graded till the last week of the quarter, because of this It was hard to get feedback. The final project got changed to a exam 2.5 weeks before finals. There was inconsistencies in the class at times and the syllabus got changed twice.

Scott is a great professor. This class was mostly good, there were a couple topics that felt a little rushed but it still wasn't too bad. He has very clear grading criteria and he explains things very well in lectures. Be prepared for some group assignments. If you show up to class and pay attention you'll probably do fine overall.
rating_tags: Group projects--Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

review_text: He is very passionate and knows what he's talking about. You can get good grades by showing up to class and do the in-class problems for completion credit. His tests are tough tho, I went from an A to B+ after the midterm and final lol

Assignments are hard, but doable. This class takes a lot of time so be prepared to work on this class outside of class. I recommend going to office hours for Scott and the TA's! Super helpful and accessible.
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: Smart guy who knows his stuff but socially awkward and immature. Which is a tough combo for being a teacher. Would get visible annoyed with class when they were unresponsive to his boring azz lectures/ assignments or jokes. Really hard to listen to. If you can you should avoid.

Scott is great *almost* all around. This was the first time DATA311 was taught by anyone so it was a bit of a mess. There was quiz issues, grading difficulties, miscommunication, and failed content structuring. However as long as youre somewhat competent you should get an A.

If you haven't coded before like me this class is really hard. It is fast paced and you don't get much time to practice one subject before being taught another. My brother taught me how to do most of my assignments, without him I would've failed. The prof is nice but I felt like he didn't know exactly how to handle a remote class.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring

review_text: Really hard to listen to and blatantly does not know how to connect with students. But I mean its a CS course so what do you really expect. CSCI 141 is tough for a 100 level course.
One of the best online classes I've taken. Clear criteria for assignments, easy to understand lectures, and just a good guy. Pay attention and care about the work you do, and you'll be okay.

Yeah noticed he has a 4/5 and that's just not representative of his teaching. Lot of the highs must be from those that haven't had more college experience. He has poor lectures and his exams are not representative of the material. He once said "there will be no coding on exams" next exam had PAGES of coding. one of my 2 C's at WWU.

(I had him for 141 too)I find Scott's lecture style really endearing and while I see a lot here about him not conveying information well, he really did try to make sure you got it, and is imo willing to try explaining things a different way. If you engage earnestly with his class there's a TON for you to takeaway from it at any level of CS know-how

Scott was a great teacher, especially during this pandemic. Lectures were always fun to attend/watch and he was very enthusiastic about what he was teaching. He explains all the concepts very well. The projects are a little difficult, but by the end of them you feel very confident in what code you wrote and why it all works. No complaints
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: The class concepts themselves are not too difficult but Scott made them harder to understand than they needed to be. Assignments were very convoluted and vague in their instructions. TA's were very accessible and helpful. The one time I went to his office hours to ask questions I just ended up leaving with even more. 3/10
Really hard class, except to put a ton of time into each homework project, and hope you get a good partner.
Great professor. Engaging lectures, challenging but doable assignments, and a great personality. He does cool research too!

I had no experience in programming, so this class was difficult for me. When I told Scott I was struggling, he actually linked me up with a mentor, which was life changing to be honest. Scott truly cares about the well being of his students and wants them to succeed. His office hours are pretty busy though, so it's best to set an appointment.

CSCI 480 is the hardest class at WWU. Scott gave us six homework assignments that took a substantial amount of time (40+ hours each). Class average was a C for every homework. I once missed class for being sick, that day we formed groups for a project, the next day Scott called me out in front of the entire class calling me irresponsible.

A great intro CS teacher. Just show up to lecture and take notes and you'll be in better shape than most. He's really nice and tries to make lecture as engaging as possible while teaching things at a nice pace. He also asks for teaching feedback often, which is nice. Def would take again

This class is pretty easy if you have any coding experience. I have heard that it can be difficult without any coding experience, but there are a lot of opportunities to work with either Scott or one of the TA's to learn more. If you don't procrastinate on the labs, this is an easy A

WWU is lucky to have him there because it is not always that a college find gems like he is. He is enthusiastic about teaching and is excited about the subject. He also understands that coding is not easy for everyone and makes sure he is available as much as possible for help. He answers piazza's questions and emails fast. Super recommended.

Scotts a great professor and has a great passion for Computer Science. The only thing I didnt like is he sometimes used class time to push his political beliefs but otherwise he was one of the best professors I ever had.

A really funny teacher who has amazing lectures. Some of the homework assignments were a litttle tricky and might not have been covered in lecture but other than that if you go to the lectures you will def pass. exams were a little hard but if you know the basics youre fine

Took Scott's class for his first time teaching it and I loved it! He explains everything with great detail so the material is clear. While attendance isn't mandatory, there is usually a short quiz (1 or 2 questions) about the material that was covered, but it accounts for very little of your grade. If you want to learn Python, take Scott's class!

His lectures are on their way to good but not there yet. Condescending person for folks new to CSCI. Can't convey information well so note taking is hard. Assignments and some labs were way too vague and for assignments being most of your grade it's not a good time. Too much work for the credit load and you won't actually learn much from the class
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things--Tough grader

review_text: If you're in his class you're going to be drowning in code. Unfortunately this is the nature of 241. This guy is an AWESOME lecturer but his class is a lot of work. You will be living in the labs for the duration of his course.

Professor Wehrwein shows genuine enthusiasm for Computer Vision. Both the lectures and the projects were incredibly interesting. The projects were difficult, but Wehrwein was helpful answering students' questions and work was graded fairly. This was one of my favorite CS courses.

Professor Wehrwein although strict, grades fairly. Each assignment has a rubric that he follows as if they were the commandments for his course. Beware of his optional enhancements because they're not so "optional".
His projects are very difficult and sometimes hard to follow. But given enough time they aren't impossible. He has lots of quizzes for easy points and his labs aren't that bad either.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Clear grading criteria--Tough grader

review_text: The biggest difficulty in assignments was figuring out what he wanted us to do based on his vague and confusing instructions. He would often rush through complex topics and expect us to have a deep understanding of them on the tests, which were incredibly difficult. Very poor understanding of student comprehension.

Great guy, humorous and the slides in class are a great help. Honestly did a great job at teaching for his first time and I thoroughly understood the topics because of the way he taught. His class is hard so don't expect an easy A, but if you want to fully understand the content he will do a good job. Mandatory labs, 4 projects, weekly quizzes.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Scott Wehrwein was definitely a great professor for this class! His lectures were always interesting, entertaining and had a good sense of humor. The assignments and labs were very difficult at times and took long hours in the computer lab but that's what you have to expect in this field. Overall I highly recommend taking him if you're in CS

For this being Prof Wehrwein's first time teaching, he did a great job. He used powerpoint in lecture which made things a lot easier to visualize and understand. Lab attendance is mandatory but they're fairly easy. The 4 assignments are hard but doable and line up with lecture topics, he holds office hours and he is extremely helpful in them.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Yudong is pretty hard to follow, and doesn't make a beginner coding class very beginner-friendly. She's rather quiet and doesn't have a good handle on what she's teaching, despite being very capable and a brilliant individual. If you're new to coding, I'd suggest finding another professor for 141.
Absolute nightmare

CSCI141 uses a "flipped classroom" setup, which means you do the work outside of class and then review during the class period. Coming to class was essentially useless but required for your grade, most of the class period is free time. The exams were basically identical to the practice tests which was nice.

Class content wasn't very difficult. All the learning was from lecture videos online. You would watch a video and then fill out a worksheet. In class you would turn in a version of the worksheet with a small group and then sit there doing nothing while the professor mumbled for half an hour. Eachexam was nearly exactly the same as the practicetest

Professor Liu uses a flipped classroom setup, which basically means that all learning is done from Covid-era online lectures that we then talk about in class. She does not help you work through problems and expects you to know the answers without help at all times. Also often does not give partial credit for things on exams, leading to low scores.

Flipped classrooms don't work for this course because its a large class and only a 50 minute class period. Weird/unproductive class structure. 5 minute group assignment, then we are supposed to start POTD in class while also paying attention to the lecture and collaborating with our groups at the same time. Prof Yudong also speaks very quietly.

I learned more from Kahn Academy in 20 minutes than I did in the first two weeks of this class. Awful instructor, not really an instructor cuz she has a flipped class. This class is akin to purgatory.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Participation matters

review_text: Started using the 'flipped classroom' format in CS141. They essentially require you to teach yourself with videos of previous professors and the textbook, then come to class to work on group projects. Attendance is mandatory because of said group projects. They're a very nice person when interacting one on one, but the teaching style isn't helpful
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Lecture heavy

review_text: My tuition money was robbed from this class. It feels like high school again where I am just being babysat for a grade. The flipped class makes it seem like Yudong is leveraging her Ph.D for some extra cash. Grading is not clear, instruction is non-existent. Save yourself some time and money, wait for next quarter and take a different class.

Yudong is a smart and caring professor. Unfortunately, she misunderstands students and fails to convey complex subjects. Much of class time was spent in silence after a question where no one knew the answer. Her participation assignments were due at odd times, causing students to miss free points. Some said that this was their worst class at WWU.
rating_tags: Caring--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: 301 is not an easy class, however, Liu Yudong is a very good professor. She very well blends group work with lecture and is always looking for feedback to improve her teaching. Very helpful outside of class and has flexible office hours.
It was her first time teaching this class, so I don't want to judge too hard.

Overall an alright class, but it felt like we were learning the material along with her. I would have liked more structure to each lecture and more responsibility taken by her to teach the material to us as opposed to students teaching the class. This being said, Yudong is very kind, and truly cares about your learning if you take the initiative.

I hope you like losing tons of credit over tiny mistakes.   This teacher is great for teaching the material but a horrible, horrible grader. like, be ready to lose 20% of an exam over a tiny mistake like missing a parenthesis. This happened multiple times, she graded a group assignment and knocked of 50% for adding 2 extra words on a proof.

By far the best CS professor I've had at WWU so far. 301 is a difficult class, but she made these concepts easier to understand. She would work out problems on the whiteboard with the class instead of just reading off slides, and most of the time we would solve questions with her. The workload is manageable and the class isn't too code heavy.
Very good lecturer, it's very probable I found her more difficult simply because the subject is extremely difficult to comprehend on its own

Yudong is great. Low ratings because she teaches a CS premajor weed-out class, but she has excellent  lectures; for my class she only asked us to either watch one of her lecture videos or read in prep for class (where she mostly solved problems on the board.) One written assignment a week and a lab. Tests were very similar to the optional homework.

Very straightforward to take notes from lectures. Appreciates students who try hard and will tell you exActly what to expect on exams if you're there to hear it. Wants students to ask questions in class and in office hours. She set up a new office hours time for me before the final because I had work.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Amazing lectures

review_text: Doesn't explain things clearly. Often times concepts get tested which are in the textbook, but were not discussed in class. The class material is very rigid / structural, which makes it hard to follow her lectures which can often be ambiguous. DISCLAIMER: This was summer quarter (5 weeks, not 10). A full quarter may alleviate these issues.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Test heavy--Tough grader

review_text: The assignments she gave were tough, and so be prepared to put hours into those. She's a tough grader also, but she's good about helping as much as she can and answering questions, so reach out if you are struggling! I've had the chance to talk with her over zoom and she's super nice and approachable. Professor Yudong is a solid professor overall.

She makes you learn a lot but sometimes her classes just don't seem worth it. Her grading is very strict and you can lose a lot of points for small errors. Her finals are very challenging and I would say if you have a heavy workload stay away from her classes.

I liked all the reinforcing daily quizzes, weekly assignments. Like other CS classes, labs were disconnected from class. If you learn the basics in Lab 1, the rest is problem solving. One complaint is that you're often expected to learn new material on your own, pre-lecture, and then watch the prof go over class quiz submissions the next day.
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Lecture heavy--Tough grader

review_text: Struggled to explain the topics well over online lectures. The process used to do/turn in homework added potentially hours to assignments. I learned most of the course material with the other students assisting and reading the textbook. Small errors in the assignments can lead to large point drops. Don't take while classes are online.

Expectations on assignments were often ambiguous, so many points were lost for seemingly minor things. Non-intuitive lectures left classmates and I feeling confused and frustrated, requiring frequent self-teaching from the textbook and online resources. Programming concepts not taught in lecture, so lab assignments were a real headache.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: Hard subject, but Yudong does a great job breaking it down. Shes really caring and great at helping with anything youre struggling with if you just ask. I really enjoyed having her as a computer science prof

Very helpful during office hours. The class itself is not difficult and she offers a lot of extra credit throughout. Very caring professor, she extended the deadline on multiple occasions and was also very eager to help.
Yudong is a great professor! Seems to be regarded as difficult but I would put that on the courses they teach not their teaching ability. Definitely would take a class from them again!

Pros: Solid coverage of the material and lots of active learning during class. Is really caring and understanding if you show the effort. Cons: She can be unclear sometimes and I wish she had some programming assignments Her midterm and final are pretty hard but fair. Overall she's a solid choice if you're willing to put the effort

She's aight. Does a bunch of examples and works through a worksheet for each topic in class. At first I thought she was kinda rude, but I don't thinks she means to be. She was really supportive and caring in office hours. Her lectures sometimes can fall a bit flat or be confusing but for the most part she's solid
Yudong is great! She has definitely improved from her past feedback. Very helpful in office hours and a very fair grader.

I didn't do very well in the class, that was my fault. However, her lectures left a bit to be desired. She could be hard to understand at times, and the questions she asked to the class were unclear at best, and she would get frustrated when nobody could answer. Overall not a terrible teacher, just nothing fantastic.
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Skip class? You won't pass.--Lecture heavy

review_text: Yudong is willing to help you when you're struggling. Opens her office hours if they don't work with your schedule and usually responds to emails within 2 hours.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring

review_text: If you do the homework and check your work carefully, you can do well in this class. Prepares you well for tests, and doesn't weight them too heavily (a good class for people who aren't great at taking tests but are hard workers).
rating_tags: Participation matters--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Lots of little assignments. If you do the work, you will be well prepared for the quizzes. Homework is the most important part of the grade. If you do the work, you will get a good grade.
rating_tags: Participation matters--LOTS OF HOMEWORK--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Her lectures are all over the place and very hard to follow. She asks strange extremely vague questions to the class and then frustrated when nobody knows how to answer. In her office hours she quickly grew impatient with me when i had trouble understanding her, i also found the way she spoke to me insulting, questioning my dedication to the class
rating_tags: Tough Grader--Get ready to read--Participation matters

review_text: Is all over the place during lectures. Going to class will just make you more confused, the book is much more clear. Not willing to go out of her way to be helpful to struggling students. If you go into office hours she makes it feel like you're wasting her time. Does all her lecture notes on a piece of paper that ends up covered in scribbles.

This class was entirely self Taught. The professor Yudong is by far the worst professor I have ever had. If you have this professor then you better have lots of outside help because you're going to need it!

Yudong is OK. She gives fine lectures, but her accent makes it hard to understand and she often goes over wrong examples first. This leads to some confusion. I found her office hours helpful, but when I made appointments - she would say a time worked and then when I got there would ask to have it 30 minutes later, or just wouldn't be there at all.
Great professor.  English is not her first language, but she tries hard to be clear.  Very helpful and knowledgeable, although a little disorganized at times.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Participation matters--BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES

review_text: I would highly discourage anyone of taking a class with her. She communicates poorly, grades harshly and doesn't seem to understand the subject matter herself. The most difficult aspect of the class is not the material but rather understanding her expectations. I will not take her again.

She's not a good lecturer, but she does know what she's talking about and she's available for lots of help outside of class. Take her suggested reading seriously and understand them, and you'll do fine.

Try to have one on one conversations with her as much as possible if you get stuck. Taking a class with her requires a lot of independent studying. She is there to help if you have specific questions, but you are mostly on your own or with your classmates. Communication is key with her. E-mail, office hours, whatever. Whatever you must.
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring

review_text: Horrible professor, can't teach worth a damn. I can count on one hand the number of lectures that were actually worth going to. The rest were either useless or actively harmful and confusing. She was not able to answer her own final review questions, and the answer key she posted was incomplete. Office hours were not helpful.

The assignments are vague and way above the difficulty level for an elementary cs class and lectures are unclear and difficult to follow.  I learned more in the week she had a grad student teach than from an entire quarter of her.  It was funny to watch him fix her mistakes and reteach concepts she had botched.  AVOID AT ALL COSTS.

Very reasonable grader. Lecture could be both more clear and more concise. I wonder if she sometimes throws out her lesson plan 30 minutes before class and just decides to teach something else. Assignment problems are usually interesting.  Would take again.
Encourages group discussions.  Will not spoon feed all of the answers and expects critical thinking. 3 simple projects implementing  OO techniques. Fair grader.

Good prof for 305, as it is very 'hand wavy' and more theoretical. She tends to not write out entire ideas on the board and will write down student answers that are incorrect, but then not clearly correct them. This causes some confusion in lectures. Overall shes a good 305 professor, reading the book helps and a background in math.

I think the criticism she has received here is unfair.  She was very clear in her lectures and her coding examples were very helpful.  If you have a hard time understanding her, it's probably because you didn't read the book before coming to lecture.  Her accent is not the problem.

Decent lecturer, very poor test writer. I always went to class and it really helped boost my grade, however when it came to the midterm and final, it took some skill to figure out what she was asking or implying. When confronted about the confusion she changes the subject and never addresses any concerns.
Super helpful explanations. Didn't use the book at all, but going to every class got me an A.

Yudong is probably one of the worst CS professors I've had. The topics in this class were super trivial, but she often chose the worst way possible to teach them. Some labs were too easy, while others too hard. Exams were full of tricks and poorly-worded questions. Expect to have a bad time.
Good Professor! Book is extremely useful. Lectures are a good reinforcement of the info. Labs and projects are appropriate level. She is helpful in office hours.

Difficult to understand, Gives lots of false information. Lots of unclear questions on tests that are difficult to interpret because her English isn't super great. Hold out for a better prof if you can

Teaches without a book, does not know ADA, can't answer questions when asked and will instead start questioning the students and even then not consistant with answers. Contradictory in test questions, tell you one thing in class but marks it as wrong on exam. Unhelpful with assignments and EXTREMELY VAGUE exams, will not clarify incorrect answers
Taught the wrong material for 5 weeks and only noticed the mistake when some of the students complained.  I like her personally, but she isn't a great teacher.

Very nice lady, awful teacher. Spends half the lecture scribbling code and incomprehensible diagrams on the board, very little time explaining. Often times she is rather condescending in class or in office hours, although I don't think she means to be. Doesn't know Ada. Class average on her tests was 60%, she didn't curve it. Avoid avoid avoid.
She is rather unhelpful in office hours. Her assignments are unclear and she grades too hard. Avoid taking her class if you can. After many computer science courses, this was the worst.

After taking several courses with her, I think she does not deserve much of the criticism that she receives. With that said, I think her tests are quite difficult (often too difficult), though she is willing to help with the subject matter during office hours. I honestly think she just needs more constructive criticism to help improve her classes.

Really difficult to follow her lectures. She goes through them way too fast and she expects you to write every little thing down so you have no time to try and comprehend what she is trying to say. Not much flexibility with time extensions. Everyone seemed to be struggling with the material.
Will not take a class with her again.  Worst professor I have ever had.  A rock would make a better professor, at least it wouldn't waste my time.
Not helpful in office hours. Struggles with ada, asks the students questions when she is in doubt which is almost always.
She is new to teaching but knows material really well and explaines it pretty clear. Whenever I had a qustion, she would take her time to explain.

This teacher just reads off slides that have been copied from the book which can be found free online, and way more helpful. She doesn't understand questions in class, or shoots people down when they answer wrong. She doesn't really help during office hours either, just makes it more confusing. WAIT to take this class at all costs if you can.

I learned far more by doing research online than going to her confusing lectures. As if the language barrier didn't make things hard enough, most of the time it seems like she doesn't know what she's talking about to begin with. I was planning on taking 347 next quarter but since she is teaching it I decided to hold off.

Awful teacher. Gives homework that is extremely hard and impossible to do without help, which she does not give in class. In class, she reads off her lecture slides and when a student asks a question, she tries to answer it for 10 minutes. Waste of time.

Generally pretty rude and stares. He can be very condescending and is not understanding or approachable. If you can survive the intense quarter-long project, you will learn a lot. However, the project is assigned in increments every week (a lot of hours per week), and if you don't do well one week you're screwed. Be a robot for this class.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

review_text: Get ready to put in twice as much work as other 4 credit CS classes. Test questions are poorly designed, encouraging mindless memorization of obscure facts as opposed to understanding concepts. Avoid this guy.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Test heavy

review_text: This class was one of the more difficult I've taken in the CS program, but incredibly fair. Phil is clearly very knowledgable and like a CS wizard, and if you can show up to lectures and put in the work outside of class, you will learn so much. He's also often available to help outside of class in his office.
The projects that Phil assigns can be extremely difficult, but he was able to teach me more in one quarter than I learned in the rest of the CS program.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: His assignments are crazy hard and way too long, be prepared to spend 10+ hours a week outside of class on them. Also exams are rough, average is 60-70, though he curves it doesn't really help. He's nice and willing to help but compared to other professors his class is way too difficult.

You will learn a lot in Phil's 347. The tests are rough, but are curved by dividing by the highest score. The homework assignments are also tough, but you are given ample time and can turn them in late. He is super helpful in office hours and his lectures are engaging.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Test heavy

review_text: This class was above and beyond the hardest class I've taken so far. You will have absolutely no free time if you take this class. Easily spent over 20 hours a week on homework a week with extremely confusing assignment documentation that were constantly getting edited just days before the assignment was due. average test scores were 60% as well.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Test heavy--Accessible outside class

review_text: Dr. Nelson is incredibly knowledgeable and a very good teacher. If you are willing to put in 15-20 hours of work outside of class, you will learn a LOT. If you don't put in the time it is easy to fall behind, so he may not be the professor for everyone. Probably the best professor I've had when it comes to giving extra support outside of lectures.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: Nelson is comically incompetent as a teacher and should not still be teaching. He actively assigns unreasonable work and seems to take delight in the difficulty of his classes, which are mainly due to having to deal with his teaching.

Clearly passionate about CS but assigns 20 hour homework projects every week and used us as guinea pigs for a new curriculum. The tests are very conceptual and extremely memorization heavy without any notes allowed, and make up 45% of your grade. The homework projects are interesting and help with understanding.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Graded by few things

review_text: I will say Dr. Nelson does give incredible lectures, they are super engaging to listen to. However. The tests questions are based on memorization in most cases, no notes, with about 20 questions being multiple choice and 5 short answer. Also be ready to devote all of your time to homework, with assignments taking 20+ hours with a week turnaround.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework--Amazing lectures

review_text: Professor Nelson does not respect your time. A 30 - 50 page assignment every week is just absurd, and some have taken me over 20 hours. Completely sucked all my time and energy away every week. This class could be just as effective with half the workload, and I know other professors who teach the same class are not like this.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: Phil is hard to work with, expects you to know so much about a topic never taught before, Tells you very high level content and expects you to instantly be able to write code for that. Huge disconnect between class and test and the massive projects due every week
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Get ready to read--Graded by few things

review_text: Phil is an amazing professor. He knows his stuff. That said, compilers kicked my butt. It was scarily easy to fall behind and from about the middle of the quarter on I was constantly playing catch-up because his lectures are so dense. Take 450 + other easier classes because the project is incredibly time consuming.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: I waited a year to take this class, and boy, was it worth it. Dr. Nelsons lectures on the subject were engaging and packed with useful content. He was available and helpful in office hours. Fair warning, this course is difficult. Probably the hardest I've taken at Western. Cool project, cool class.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Amazing lectures

review_text: Probably the hardest class I've taken at WWU. Creating a compiler is incredibly hard, and for some reason no matter how much time I put into it I wasn't able to make enough progress. Phil knows his stuff and could easily go on for hours just talking about compilers. The compiler is a huge time commitment, so don't take anything hard with this class
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: If you take a class from Dr. Nelson, be prepared. He expects a lot out of his students, and with homework taking 20+ hours to complete his classes can be a real time drain. But, he really cares for his students. He seems to be always be available to get help from, and the knowledge of this man knows no bounds! Truly one of a kind, but VERY HARD.

Phil embodies hard but fair. Attendance is not graded and lectures are recorded. Tests are difficult with tight time limits but are open book. Do the written questions first it helps a ton. Assignments are difficult and build off each other so start early. He's very available for outside help which you will need often. Very clear expectations

Lectures were good although most of the time in this class is working on your assignment make sure you start early. Don't take heavy classes with this class. He expects a lot from you. It's a hard class but you learn a lot.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Amazing lectures

review_text: Professor Nelson expects a lot out of his students, but that's a good thing. I learned more in this class alone than I learned from any other CSCI class at western. His projects time consuming, but they truly help you to understand the topics in class. He is quick to respond to emails and seems to enjoy helping students, so don't hesitate to ask.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: Phil Nelson's class is the most valuable course I have taken at western. I learned so much from this man.

In lecture Phil gave a lot of insight on the core ideas of mobile development, and the 3 hw taught you what you needed to know, even if you ended up just looking up how to program things in Android online. He hadn't read the textbook for this course ahead of time, and the last time he used Android was 4.0 which made it hard, but it wasn't his fault
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Accessible outside class--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: He's really helpful and knowledgeable, but it's nearly impossible to understand his lectures unless you've already read the textbook and know all the terminology and jargon he liberally throws around. Which means you're basically gonna be self-studying.

Very good professor, he's always available for help and makes sure you really understand the course material. Assignments are hard, but useful and satisfying to complete. Tests are also difficult, but the final grade was curved.

This guy expects the world from you, so I hope you aren't taking a full load of classes. His assignments are very challenging and you only have a little over a week for each one. average on his tests are around 60% which he thinks is fine. He will take the top grade as the new A in the class at the end and curves stuff. It will be be rough.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--Get ready to read--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: BEST PROF IN CS DEPARTMENT! Very straight forward test questions, no tricks or things you dont learn from going to class and doing the assignments. Assignments build on each other. He is almost always available, even at 3am Sunday morning or morning before its due, 9 times out of 10 he will respond with some insight which is profoundly helpful.

Lectures can get a bit monotonous but if you can pay attention you will learn a ton from him. The projects are difficult and can be time consuming but he is very receptive to student questions both through emails and office visits. He definitely knows his stuff and wants you to do well.

The class is pretty hard, but for the wrong reasons. The material is manageable, but he assigns homework like his students don't have other obligations, making it hard to turn in quality work. his tests are mostly memorizing info from the man pages, instead of problem solving or critical thinking. Lectures dry and likely without revision for years.
Tough as nails class. Inflexible on his own rubric but typically helpful. Definitely the smartest professor I've had to date.

I was terrified to take UNIX because of it's notoriety in being difficult, but it ended up being the most useful and favorite class of the CS major. Nelson goes above and beyond to help out in office hours and will answer emails at any hour. Start assignments day-of, go to class, study hard and you'll get a solid grade.
Unix is difficult but mostly only if you try to cram the assignments. Give yourself 3 days and work on it each day. No problem but a lot of time spent.

He's a great professor that expects a lot out of his students. The hw and tests might be hard, but he's always available for help and if you make it into class everyday he will tell you how to succeed in the class.

Unix is a hard class, but Dr. Nelson makes it doable by always being on hand to answer questions in a very clear, helpful way, no matter how simple the question may seem. A lot of homework is assigned, but all seems useful, and it's graded fairly.

Awesome professor.  It's a lot of work, but well worth it for what you learn.  Be prepared to work hard in his classes.  Although his tests are difficult, if you sincerely understand the material you'll be fine.  I marked attendance as not mandatory since he doen't keep attendance, but in order to do well on tests it's pretty important to show up.

For any of his classes--OOP, Unix, Compilers, OSes--you better work your butt off!  I used to think BSing though classes was the way to go, and it can be beneficial to get through the "stupid" classes, but Nelson's classes teach you discipline and determination cause mark my words you will suffer the wrath of STRUGGLE if you want to pass and learn!

I don't know what it really was but I never felt comfortable at all in Nelson's class or trying to approach him with questions. His presentation style with slides is helpful in some ways but he doesn't really go through too much on the board to help get the concept across. He does provide lots of sample code online though which is very helpful.

Really tough class, and you'll get through it only if you sincerely understand the material. There's no "faking your way through" here, either on tests or assignments. Prof Nelson is awesome about going the extra mile to help/explain in study sessions, office hours, and email at all hours!

Great teacher incredibly helpful especially if you go in and talk with him in office hours. Unfortunately if you havent already been programming for years youll be really confused. Homework assignment are excruciatingly long and complicated and you spend way to much time trying to figure out C++ on your own since you don't really learn it in class.

He's a cool guy, sometime funny. He expects a lot and assignments are hard if you don't know C. He has a stupid policy test policy, you need to get 50% average on all of his test to pass the class and his test are quite tricky. Cool class if you have lots of free time.

Definitely knows his stuff. Nice guy and extremely helpful in office hours.  His online slides showing his lectures are only bullet points, however, so looking back on them later is close to useless. Also, assigns quite a bit of work for a 3 credit class.  Still, learned a lot.  Just a tough class.

Nelson is amazing. He's practically a genius, but very down to Earth. The guy answers emails at 2 in the morning in the couple of days leading up to a deadline. Programs are challenging, but you learn a lot in his class. Tests are brutal - part multiple choice, part short answer. But his multiple choice are pretty tough. He does curve, though, so i

This guy's a ****. He's really laid back, he knows the subject well, he's enthusiastic, and REALLY down to earth. Assignments were long, and semi-difficult, (challenging but manageable if you make time), but I learned a lot from them. I definitely recommend Dr. Nelson. He's a great teacher.

The middle part of the class focusing on IP, TCP, and UDP protocols was fairly inresting, but the beginning of the class spent too much time covering obvious facts concerning transfer mediums (several assigned problems dealt with manually calculating satellite transmission delays, we get it, satellite has a several second delay...) and the last par

Good class.  Learned alot about Unix and development in Unix.  Programming assignments were also interesting (6 cumulative assignments building a simple minishell).  Tests are a bit too detailed oriented, but he does grade on a curve.  Greate class but involves a lot more work than a 3 credit class should require.  Should be 4-5 credits.

probably one of the hardest teachers in the department.  The assignments take a very long time but are all programming assignments.  The tests are tricky, in that the questions all ask about small details and not just the broad topics.  Really nice guy in general.  Says "Along the way" a lot

The subject matter is pretty interesting, unfortunately he requires all the assignments to work under UNIX, but hardly ever discusses UNIX programming. He is also extremely slow at returning assignments, so slow that even now that the quarter is over I still do not know how I did on the last 5 out of 8 assignments.

His classes are a lot of work, but are extremely practical.  Very project orieneted, you write software that actuially does something, rather than stupid little exercises.  For instance in 352 we wrote a UNIX shell, and in 450 we implemented a compiler.  In 513 we wrote device drivers for an A/D PCI card.
Learned a lot in this class.  He is a bit lax in grading and returning assignments and tests.
No Comments
Very interested in the subject material.  Went the extra mile to try and make up for some hardware defencies we experienced.

This man has been teaching here for years and you would think he would have ironed out his teaching...oh no. He doesn't turn assignments back until months after they were turned in, letting you guess how well you are doing. He can be pretty nice though if you go to his office and ask.

It was surreal being in a classroom where you could feel the conglomerate frustration and confusion at the professor's ability to teach. The repeated stress of trying to meet milestones of a group project, to then having it extended the next day in class after complaints that we were not taught the material, was unbearable. Worst experience at WWU.
Lectures were frustrated. Quizzes/exams were random trivia. Grading criteria was absurd. Quarter long project taught me the material but took way too much time and was difficult to do with a partner.

Taking this class with Prof. Sharmin was not an experience I can recommend to anyone. Lectures are extremely frustrating, and it is very difficult to get an answer to any questions you might have. Do not expect to get grades back for at least 3 weeks in this class, and any feedback you might get back is borderline useless. Avoid this course.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Group projects--Graded by few things

review_text: Unclear assignments and grading rubrics. Answers to questions are not helpful and don't answer the question.

This class wasn't my favorite. The lectures never felt really useful and I had to teach myself the concepts before going into the lab. I had a good time working on the assignments, they were challenging but satisfying to complete. The biggest issue I have with this prof is the exams. There's only 2, but they were worth the majority of your grade.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Lectures were well-organized and informative. Expectations were communicated clearly. Lab work was interesting and challenging. Would recommend and/or enroll again.

Terrible teacher, just bad at her job. Seems spiteful when the class struggles. Your grade does not reflect effort or understanding. Impossible to pass the midterm, she has no interest in seeing any of her students succeed in CS. Can't believe I paid for this. Doesn't pick favorites, she dislikes all her students equally.

Basically everything I don't like about profs at wwu. Terrible lectures, you learn almost nothing, way too much busywork but somehow also not graded by many things, unclear project instructions + grading rubrics, pop quizzes, tests that are just random trivia from the slides instead of actual applicable skills, the works.  Avoid.
rating_tags: Beware of pop quizzes--Graded by few things

review_text: I took Dr Sharmin for 145 and 330. She was a lot better than I thought she would be based on the general rmp reviews. She honestly was very helpful and acessible when you needed her. If you stay on top of what's going on and ask questions you'll do fine. I had issues with my partner for the final project and she as very understanding and helpful.
rating_tags: Group projects--Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: A professor that I can tolerate most of the time. As someone who has already coded I'm java, most of the class felt like a breeze. However, the two exams I did on paper did not feel like they reflected my actual skills. I somehow aced the midterm by sheer dumb luck and the final singlehandedly dropped my grade by a letter.

Lectures are okay, labs and projects were somewhat challenging but weren't too much of a problem. Her exams are terrible. There are only two, the midterm and the final, and they are responsible for most of your grade. They're given on pencil and paper (no, I'm not joking, I wish I was) and are much more challenging than the practice exams.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: If you've never coded in Java, or are new to coding concepts in general, you're likely going to struggle through this class. In class explanations are mostly confusing unless you have some pre existing schema on the given topic. It's possible to succeed in this class as a new coder, but you'll have to attend lecture and study topics independently.

She teaches logic, not code. In-class code is done by her on the whiteboard and you aren't expected to follow along on your computer. Midterm and final are on paper which is ridiculous. The practice final exam had questions about information we didn't go over in class. Midterm practice was not as hard as the actual midterm.

The lecture videos were not very helpful. A lot of the time it felt like we were having to teach ourselves. Theres been poor communication all quarter. We were left in the dark on whether the midterm would be curved at all when the average was 53%. We also found out after the quarter ended that canvas gradebook was actually inaccurate all quarter.

Teaching over zoom is difficult but it also means that being able to communicate with students over email is especially important. Sharmin did not do this well at all. She ignored multiple emails from students and the email she sent after the midterm felt condescending considering how much effort the class put into preparing for the midterm. Avoid
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Lecture heavy--Tough grader

review_text: Dr. Sharmin is a professor to avoid. As much as we can give a pass for inconsistent grading, unclear lectures, and heavy homework load what we can't overlook is her unprofessional attitude towards students. She will blame us, her colleagues, or her curriculum rather than take responsibility. 350 words is not enough to say how awful this class was.

I really wanted to give Sharmin a chance. I had really high hopes for her from what I heard from upperclassmen. I also truly believe that she is not a bad person, however - long story short, over 80% of the class failed her midterm. Made empty promises and ended up half-delivering. Lectures are unhelpful, expectations unclear. Avoid.

I feel like professor Sharmin got a bad rap this quarter. She had a hard time preventing cheating during the final and midterm and the way she conducted the test made a lot of students mad. it might've been annoying but not worth throwing a fit over. I have heard from many people she is great for upper lever cs classes. Maybe not for 145 though.

She is the worst. I did well on every assignment and the final, thinking I received an A in the course, and then she decides to include the midterm where everyone failed (due to lack of time, bad format) last minute in our final grades with MINIMAL curving. The whole class felt like a bad prank. Don't take unless you would like to be screwed over.
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Test heavy--Tough grader

review_text: This class was just one mess after another. The quarter started with bad links, incorrect information and unclear assignments. Has recorded lecture videos then repeats the same thing in class, both of which are unhelpful in learning material. Better off with google. Teacher is uninterested in student well being and is VERY disorganized. Skip her!

This was by far my least favorite class I've taken so far at Western. Her lectures were super boring and not very helpful so I ended up having to teach myself most things. I don't recommend taking this class if you don't have any prior experience with Java and I recommend avoiding this professor if you can.

I second all of the review from Mar 3rd 2021 CSCI 145. In addition, despite the class trying to figure out if a curve/retake/or other solution would be offered for the low midterm which failed us all. The problem was brushed off with passive aggressive replies until finals week leaving the class in distress the entire second half of the quarter.

This prof will ruin your experience for CS if youre not prepared for learning Java on your own. The class average for midterm was 20.8/50 and she passive aggressively said it was OUR fault for not being better at time management. Shes snarky in her emails and the labs/projects are super difficult. Skip CS for a quarter to avoid her if you can.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: From my personal experience with Dr. Sharmin, she takes user feedback seriously. Our class gave her feedback on how we really didn't like true/false questions, so for the final, she didn't have any. She covers a lot of information in such a small amount of time, it makes me wish we had two classes for this since all the info is very important.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Group projects--Clear grading criteria

review_text: 345 with Sharmin was challenging. I think that working remotely definitely added more difficulty than an in-person class would. I ended up spending more time working on Deadwood than actually learning the material, much less putting the lecture materials to use. Didn't consciously put any design patterns taught in lecture to use in Deadwood
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Group projects

review_text: Lectures tend to be all over the place and you're never really sure what she expects you to take away. Asking clarifying questions can just lead to more confusion and obfuscation of the topic. Grading criteria was completely unclear, and felt entirely subjective. Not very responsive to emails. Overall felt like I learned very little from this class

Object oriented with Moushumi was very frustrating. lectures were extremely vague and scattered and when we went over topics she would never follow up with a takeaway or right answer, she would always leave things open ended and students confused. Tested on things that were never once mentioned.

Sharmin's grading is infuriating. The questions were vague and very highly specific answers were required. If you didn't write said specific things, your score was heavily impacted. Very poor instruction, tests didn't resemble lecture material not quizes, projects were lengthy, and was called out for asking questions deemed "easonable by classmates

As someone who has never coded in java before, Sharmin was probably the worst teacher for the course. Her lectures are scattered as well as her presentation style. On top of this, her weekly quizzez are very hard, Especially if you have to take them before lab. Unless you have a lot of experience coding in java, I would avoid this teacher
rating_tags: Skip class? You won't pass.--Group projects--Tough grader

review_text: Sharmin is a definitely a professor to avoid, especially at lower levels. 145 is an intro course to get into computer science. Yet she doesn't explain topics on the labs till after their due and completely avoids topics you need for labs and tests after saying she will cover them in class. As someone with java history I struggled in this class.

Not sure why she's related so badly on here. Moushumi is exactly what you want a from a CS professor; she explains the concepts clearly in class, is helpful in her office hours, and gives good CS advice. The only complaint I could have from the class is that some of the quizzes were graded subjectively, so I recommend talking to her if it happens.

Grading is super subjective on tests and quizzes an she's not clear what she want's exactly until you get stuff back. Too many open ended questions can make you have to think until you almost run out of time. The project is cool but you will mostly be on your own. Start early because it take a lot of time to finish.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: This was a fun class because of the quarter-long group project aspect. Lectures were a little rough, and sometimes she called students on random. I never used the textbook(s) but I could see how they might be helpful.

There are pop quizzes every week, they are extremely vague and subjective and she's a tough grader making the averages 7/10 and won't curve. Also she will not post keys and isn't fast with grading. The project was fine but the tests will get you because her lectures drag on and tests are very specific.
rating_tags: Tough Grader--BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Tests are tough

review_text: Summer quarter: 10 weeks of material covered in 5 weeks is doable. Reading is not required, but I definitely recommend keeping up with it as it is so helpful in understanding the lectures. Tests have a question for each major concept, so if you aren't too strong in one subject, you aren't penalized too badly. Overall, work hard and you'll be fine
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: (Summer quarter) 12 week course condensed into 6 weeks with double the people on the project with lagging lecture time when it is needed before starting an assignment.  Too many hands with too little time with too much confusion and not enough explanation.
rating_tags: BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: I liked her, we had 3 projects, 2 were with a partner of your choosing, which worked out really well for most people. I thought her lecture was good, not incredible but i could follow along and i did not have to rely on the book to teach me. The projects were hard but doable, and she gave helpful hints. Also, her tests were really easy.

I think there is a negative representation of Moushumi because her first quarters at Western have been bumpy and inconsistent. In my opinion, she values her students and if you treat her with respect you will learn what you need moving forward and do well in the class and in the future. Admittedly, a poor lecturer. No other complaints.

Lectures are slow and all over the place. Asks students to answer questions and then proceeds without saying whether they were right or wrong, which made the class even more confusing. The final project was creating a GUI in Java for a game that already had a back-end, but she never lectured on GUI creation. Has trouble answering student questions.

The class consists of one long group project which she warned us was a "disaster" the last time it was assigned. The project is mostly busy work and she did not teach relevant object oriented principles soon enough for them to be even implemented as part of the project. Most students didn't finish the project. Don't take her unless you have to.

Communication was tough, I could barely understand her and she never seemed to actually answer/understand our questions. Made easy material way more difficult than it should have been. Constantly contradicted herself in lectures. Would call on people in class to answer super specific questions. She was more helpful one on one in office hours.

Extremely unclear lectures, quizzes and assignments. She tends to ask questions expecting a very specific answer, then proceeds to belabor the class and call on random students when she doesn't immediately receive the answer she's looking for, which tends to waste class time, as does her habit of repeating simple concepts ad nauseam .

Took Data Structures with her and she made the class much harder than it should be. Exams questions are ambiguous. If you justify your answer for  anything on an exam you can go to her office and she will give you full or partial credit back if you have a strong enough enough case. Correct answers may contradict what she taught in class. Beware.

Confusing most of the time. She was very helpful in office hours but doesn't teach very well in lectures and not great at elaborating when someone doesn't understand. Keep up with notes and comparing to the book helped loads.

Prof obviously knows what she does, but teaching is not her best ability. Communication was difficult because English is not her first language and she's trying to explain complicated math and tech terms so it ends up being her repeating the same explanation and getting frustrated that we didn't get it. Dropped my CSCI minor and stopped going.
Fun projects, difficult tests

Tough homework that requires full understanding of the material to complete. Fair tests. Lectures are great. Filip conveys the concepts very effectively and lots of work done on the whiteboard which I appreciate. I learned a a lot and appreciate the care Filip puts into each course!
Amazing professor. He is clearly passionate, and it shows each lecture,

Filip was a great professor. Very engaging and funny in lectures. He would encourage collaboration, in and outside of class, which helped understand tough concepts. I would definitely recommend taking him.

His lectures were good at first but got a lot more confusing as time went on in the class. His perimeters for homework assignments and labs were unreasonable to people new to coding. He limited the number of functions, and lines of code even though it seemed unfair to new students. He also is unhelpful when asking questions/ is very vague.

Was nice and fair with grading. Content was not too hard too understand. Spend a day learning each topic and its honestly easy. Hardest part of this class was remembering the rules for Log and the tricky wording in the tests.

Everyone seems to like Filip; I didn't have a great experience with him. He felt manipulative in how he structured the class--he actually curved down my class by making the cut-off for an A versus A- a 95 after he gave too much extra credit. Similarly he intentionally made things due too early so he could extend them and be the hero. Great
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Beware of pop quizzes--Lecture heavy

review_text: This guy's nickname is J-God for a reason. One of the best teachers around, not just at wwu. Extremely caring/understanding/helpful inside and outside of class, the guy frequently helps students get jobs at major companies and on top of all that his lectures are fantastic and his tests/grades are super fair. Incredible teacher!
Excellent teaching, very accessible and ready to help out students.

Filip is a very skilled lecturer and test-writer. Great choice for a serious CS student or anyone up for a good challenge. His classes are well designed and fair but not easy. Frequent in-class quizzes make good attendance important. As head of the department, he delegates a lot to his TAs, so he can be hard to pin down after class.

Honestly the most accommodating professor I've had so far at western, and he has so much enthusiasm for every single lesson which makes computer science engaging. He takes the intimidation away from learning your first baby steps of code.
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Beware of pop quizzes--Caring

review_text: This prof reminds me why I want to major in CSCI. His lectures were engaging, and he delivered the material with passion. The weekly labs felt like puzzles in the best sense, as we would take his concepts and actually apply them for an in depth understanding. Took this class at the start of the pandemic and he understood that life was rough for us.

I had Filip in an asynch online class, so the format was challenging, but Filip was good about communication and consistent check-ins. The lectures were not super easy to learn from for me, in the end I only read the textbook. He was also really nice in granting extensions. One or two homework assignments per week, usually not too difficult
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Filips lectures were pretty good and are straightforward. You dont even need to know a single thing about programming in order to understand all of his lectures in his CSCI 141 class. The textbook was also useful as well as it was interactive.

Without a doubt the most passionate educator at WWU CS department. He gives each student his full attention during office hours. He is also compassionate as he understands the workload this course can bring. I am lucky to have taken his class whenever I could. Indeed a real gem!

Best prof in the department, hands down. This was my third class I have taken with him, and I have enjoyed every single one. Excellent lectures, clear expectations, and very good at gauging student learning. You won't regret taking any of his classes.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Filip is by far the best professor I've had at Western so far. Friendly guy, explains things in an intuitive way, and you can tell he's passionate about what he teaches. The exams and homework assignments are all fair, and I felt like I learned a ton from his class. Two thumbs up!

Filip clearly enjoys teaching and that comes through in his lectures, which are very well done. In this class there were daily quizzes/exercises on the previous day's material, so while attendance wasn't *mandatory* per se it was if you wanted to do well. Seconding the coffee/donuts for 8AM final. Great all around.
rating_tags: Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Always available for extra help through office hours and email. Really cares about students and comprehension. Bonus- brings muffins and coffee to 8am final

Filip is the best professor of all time, his teaching style is great and he's really caring for the students. Let him help you, he knows what he's doing. He's very knowledgeable of a broad array of computer science concepts, in academia and industry. If you get him as a professor, make sure to take advantage of your money's worth, he's a gem.

The fact that 100 people are in the class does not prevent this professor from teaching everyone as effectively as if you were the only student. His daily quizzes let you know how you are doing and give you lots of practice coding on paper, which you will need throughout CS.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

review_text: In my opinion one of the best CS prof's I have had so far, really cares and is interested in the topic, lectures are super helpful and will always show what you need for lab and for exams.
rating_tags: Respected--Skip class? You won't pass.--Amazing lectures

review_text: Online textbook is dope, his lectures are good but are more on the faster side. Labs are easy enough with TA, homeworks are manageable with adequate help and time. Midterm and final were very hard. Daily "quizzes" but you can work with partner. Learned a lot and would recommend to others.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: Very good professor. Really cares about students and is super nice. The class isn't too difficult as long as you stay on top of homework assignments and do the handouts to practice. He gives extra credit opportunities and replaces your midterm score if you do better on the final. The tests are a bit hard but very fair

The best professor in the department by far! You will have short quizzes every day but can work with you neighbors.(20% final grade) and lowest 3 get dropped. You have homework assignments that are a mix of book and coding questions(40% final grade and typed in latex). The midterm and final are way harder than the sample ones so study hard.
In class quizzes almost everyday but there is always a chance to talk to neighbors.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--LOTS OF HOMEWORK

review_text: Was my favorite CSCI class I have had. Filip really cares about his students. He always extended due date, you what his extra credit was going to be on the exam and was always there during office hours. He also held extra review sessions with example problems before the mid term and final that where super helpful. If you can GO do GO!
rating_tags: EXTRA CREDIT--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Gives good feedback

review_text: Filip makes custom presentations for every lecture and they're very easy to follow. He's good at what he does and can be pretty funny at times. If I could have him for every CS course I would.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures
