review_text: Fillip is a good professor, and able to explain many concepts during lecture. The weekly homework and lab material does get increasingly more and more difficult however, so be prepared for that. He allows for the use of online resources, and I recommend using google, the python API, and stackoverflow whenever you need help.
rating_tags: Participation matters--BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Amazing lectures

review_text: This is a wonderful class if you have never touched a finger in programming, like me. Very clear lectures. The material is challenging in a way that is very intuitive and easy to understand why things work. I never read the e-book (which is provided) but I don't think it was vital anyways. Amazing course and teacher for the intro to the program.

Filip does a great job at introducing students to computer science in this class. His lectures are engaging and he is a certified beauty to work with if you go to his office hours. Homework and labs were very interesting My gripes are that we have graded quizzes almost everyday and the tests are HARD. Even compared to the sample exams he gives.
rating_tags: BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--Clear grading criteria--Tests are tough

review_text: Filip is a really good prof, he is very clear in what he expects of his students. He is helpful in office hours and his projects were easy enough too. His tests covered the material and he offered a lot of extra credit.

I'm glad I chose to take intro to programming with Filip! He makes learning the somewhat challenging material, fun! He was incredibly encouraging and understanding. He provided many resources for those struggling.
Professor Jagodzinski is one of my favorite professors at Western. His lectures are super engaging and he is really great at making sure his students understand all the concepts.

Filip gave me a great first impression of the CS program and I hope to take him again! Passionate guy, balanced lectures, all of the material and exams were challenging but doable with a sufficient amount of studying. Understanding guy that wants to make sure everyone does as well as they can in the course. Highly recommend!
rating_tags: LOTS OF HOMEWORK--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Amazing lectures

review_text: Easily my favorite teacher in the department. He genuinely values student learning. Challenging but rewarding homework/lab assignments. Put effort in and it's an easy A and you'll have learned a lot. Lectures are very clear, well structured, and starts with a short review for the first five minutes of each class. J-god for president.

Filip was a fantastic professor and a huge asset to WWU. He kept the class at a good pace, assignments were of a reasonable difficulty, and the homeworks were interesting. Tests were HARD, average for the final was a D but he brought it up to a C with a curve. Do well on the homework and labs to make up for bad test scores. Go to optional review!

First of all, he makes custom PowerPoints for each class, which means no book slides or scrolling through PDFs, like some professors. You can tell that he really wants his students to be successful in their learning. There are exam review sessions that are super helpful. The class was difficult, but passable. I would reccomend taking his class.

Filip is my favorite professor I've had so far at WWU. He really cares about each of his students and is willing to go the extra mile to make sure you succeed. Go to his exam reviews! They are super helpful and he uses some of the questions on the exam. My only gripe is it takes a 95% for an A. Easy to pass, but expect to work for an A.

Filip is a amazing teacher! He takes the time to explain solutions to any questions and never makes you feel stupid for not understanding something. Lectures are short and concise and all the powerpoints are posted in canvas for student review. Tests and homework are challenging, but there are loads of opportunities to get help and review for exams

Great professor - takes a hard concept and breaks it down into understandable pieces. Lectures are great, slides are useful, labs are great, and homework assignments are tough, but doable. Cares more about your understanding of topic. Overall 10/10 would take prof again.
Filip now works at WWU and is a fantastic teacher, would highly recommend.

Hao remains the GOAT. No tests since its an elective. All project based, lots of practice with APIs and styling. Encourages students to work with AI. Answers questions quickly and is avaliable on Discord. The only negative reviews of Hao are from people who can't handle a straight shooter or ask lots of redundant questions.
rating_tags: Group projects--Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

review_text: I am taking both 305 and 330 with Dr. Hao, and he is an amazing teacher. He explains technical knowledge with clarity and patience. If you are going to take a course with Hao, be prepared to take quizzes in every class. The quizzes are graded by participation, low-stake and help a lot with learning and preparing for the exams!

I've never had a professor so disinterested in teaching before. Half the class is doing a problem on your own and then he goes over it before making you watch an hour of video lectures. Quizzes almost every class. No good way to practice or prepare for exams/quizzes. Takes forever to grade. Avoid if you can.
rating_tags: Lecture heavy--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Hao is one of the few professors that can teach very well in the department. I took both 301 and 330 with him, and he did a great job in helping me grasp the basics and advanced knowledge in both subjects. I love the flipped 330 more than 301. Be prepared to watch the assigned lecture videos - You can tell he put good efforts to recording them!

Acts like you're stupid for asking questions or not remembering content from non-prereqs. Quizzes every day, either graded or completion, which he decides on a whim after you take it. He would rather you talk to ChatGPT than come to office hours. Assigns prerecorded lectures as daily HW. Reuses examples on tests so copy them all to your note sheet.

Hao is very aggressive and condescending to students when answering questions, I don't think this is intentional but he makes people not want to participate in class. He is also not very helpful in lab. Pop quizzes every day, usually graded on participation. Each day he posts lecture videos to watch on your own after class.

Hao was excellent at teaching this class, 301 is math heavy and he did a great job explaining it step by step.  Labs were in racket, which is a strange language but were overall quite easy. Highly recommend taking him, explained everything very clearly, didn't get sidetracked and allowed for points back on tests.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback

review_text: Took this 2022 Fall. Dr. Hao is a great professor, though the pre-major classes are difficult overall. Posts lecture slides and recordings so that you don't fully miss out, but recently been taking attendance in future courses.  Sometimes questions are hard to run by him, as the content is pretty confusing anyway. He made 241 seem less daunting.
This class was great, the professor was honest about what to expect on exams and placed an emphasis on what is actually important after college in the industry.
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Graded by few things

review_text: Hao is well versed and knowledgable. He can explain things in a palatable way and makes things easy to follow and understand. The demos that he did on the whiteboard were great. Hao cares about that you learn something for real in class, and can be off-putting if you don't do anything.

Relatively strict attendance policy. Comes off a bit abrasive in class and doesn't seem to enjoy teaching too much. Sometimes just misses or misunderstands questions entirely and overall not a fun class to be in. Least favorite instructor in Western so far.

Dr. Hao is a pretty good CS professor. Deserves maybe 4.5. He was *usually* pretty good at explaining stuff, though didn't always understand questions. The exams were intense, but the other assignments were easy, open for a long time. He had lots of code and slides to refer to.

It is clear his main goal is for students to actually learn something rather than just focus on studying for a test. One of his projects was pretty tough, but since 1/3 of the class wasn't able to complete it, he gave everyone an additional week to work on it. One of the few professors that truly cares for his students.
rating_tags: Group projects--Clear grading criteria--Accessible outside class

review_text: Professor Hao is very fair and lays out exactly what he expects from his students. He cares about his students, he gave chances to receive make up points for questions missed on the midterm.

Professor Hao is very accommodating to our needs. Several deadlines were extended. I love how clear he explains queries and E-R diagrams. All quizzes were explained and analyzed in the same week. Would definitely take again.

Hao made a lot of efforts to let us see where the database concepts get applied in the real world. He explained the core concepts clearly and is very accomodating. Hao is not a professor who you can easily get A from, but he provides valuable real world experience that helps you grow.

Hao had regular difficulty understanding the questions being asked, and would often be very rude when answering them (even though he was usually answering the wrong question). Also 70% of the final project was graded on web design, even though the class is about databases. I usually like my teachers, even the rude ones. Wouldn't take Hao again.

I had professor Hao online due to COVID-19, so my perception may be different from the norm. Hao does a good job making sure students understand *what he is talking about currently*. The two complaints I have: his rubrics for assignments were not specific enough, and for questions that didn't directly relate to the subject he would "save for later"

Professor Hao is a gem at WWU. It is abundantly clear that he truly cares about students' understanding. He breaks the classic structure of working for points and employs you to gain understanding for milestones in the future such as interviews. I felt that I was clearly being taught things that I will use in the future. I highly recommend Hao.

Dr. Hao is one of my favorite profs here. Unlike most, he actually cares about students' futures and how what they do in college will impact where they go next. He's always available for extra help and feedback, and he goes above and beyond to help students get valuable research and development experience.

This was my first quarter at Western, and Dr. Hao has been great. He always slowed down after introducing a new topic to make sure the class understood what was going on, his lectures were intuitive, and it was clear as day what would be on the midterm and final. There was even an opportunity to get points back on the midterm, which was nice.
This is a hard course. I learned a lot by going to class and doing the assignments, and that means a lot of sweat and tears. The lectures on recursion are very cool!
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Amazing lectures

review_text: Hao's tests are pretty difficult, I won't lie. I've never been a fan of having coding problems on exams as they just aren't realistic in terms of most applications except interviewing. That being said, Qiang does make a good effort at making sure you understand what's going on, although his tone could come off to some as demeaning at times.

Awesome. Cares about students and will ask how you're doing mid-lecture. Gives questions to help prep for interviews. Very understanding. Go to office hours! I didn't go that much, I regret it. Extended one project deadline when he saw the class was behind. I had no Java experience before taking 145.
Hao is a really amazing professor. The class is organized very well, but the assignments are heavy and difficult as hell.

His tests make you want to cry. He focuses a lot on problems you will see when applying for internships and jobs which gives you reality checks. His lectures can be difficult to follow. He is available outside class a lot and responds to emails quick. Lots of extra credit to make up points. Labs are hard as rocks but doable and very satisfying.
Prof Hao is a very good teacher. He's willing to listen to students and adapt his lectures. Although the attendance is not required, most of the lectures are worth going.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Awesome lectures. Slides tailored to the in-class interactions, and very effective. Appreciate the bonus points opportunities.

Doesnt teach some concepts very well. Youd better be good at understanding pseudocode quickly. If you are then youll follow lectures well. Provides his lecture slides but theyre hard to study oustide of class since they lack description. Does provide research opportunities.
rating_tags: ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: Great teacher and great person, was able to shut down people be disruptive and contstantly offered extra credit to make up for difficult tests, although the tests themselves were ALWAYS on lecture material and he was very clear with what would be on them the few classes before the test. He was very responsible to emails or questions in class.
If you go to his class and practice the given problems, you will learn a lot. Great learning experience for my first quarter at Western.
Proff. Hao's class was very well structured and he himself was very helpful when it came to questions. Would definatly reccomend!

Hao is a great prof here at WWU for beginning CS classes. His exams are exceptionally difficult (I failed both my exams); however he offers a lot of extra credit to make up for the difficult exams. The labs are very straight forward. Labs can be confusing at times, but I would not claim them to be very difficult (for the most part).
Clear and organized. I like Q's class.

This is my third CS class at Western. Failed CSCI145 in the summer, and retook it from Prof. Q this quarter. Super clear and easy to follow, 'cause every slides of his go online almost on the same day. I like the bonus assignments; really helpful in boost my grades.
rating_tags: LOTS OF HOMEWORK--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--EXTRA CREDIT

review_text: Great professor. He's super clear and well-organized. His lectures are very well-structured and full of detailed examples. The assignments are challenging but still doable. If you go to his class, you will be able to learn a lot.

One of the hardest and most informative classes I have taken. First half of the class is flipped and there are lots of collaborative pop quizzes, which sucks if other people in your group did not watch the videos. There are weekly quizzes that are tough, but don't deviate from course material. Programming assignments are hard, please start early!
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: We had weekly quizzes instead of a midterm, which I wasn't sure about but it ended up being a good check-in. This class isn't easy, but he is a great and helpful professor. The only issue I had was that we were sometimes ill-prepared for the difficult labs, but he is available for office hours and we have no other homework.

Brian expects a lot out of his students, but teaches incredibly well! He is super open to student feedback and adapts his teaching style to match that feedback.  His projects are challenging but super formative!  He holds weekly reviews outside class time for those who want another pass over the covered material.  Super accessible, and cares!

Brian is an awesome teacher. He's super knowledgeable and cares about being a good teacher. The online class was taught in a flipped format, super helpful to get hands-on practice with the concepts. The class and assignments were appropriately challenging given the subject
Easily one of my favorite professors yet, I like the format of his classes. He genuinely tries to be the best teacher he can and it shows.

The epitome of a Professor who is challenging but fair. He expects a lot of you, but if you go to all the lectures, take notes, and study you'll be fine. The projects in this class are quite large, so be weary of taking this with other work heavy classes. He explained topics very well. His programming assignments were also some of my favorite
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Dr. Hutchinson is awesome - I really wish I had not been in his class during Corona-quarter though. He made good use of the lecture times and formatted the class semi-Synchronous so it felt more evenly paced. His projects are challenging without being brutal and his grading is always fair. Solid pick.

Brian is one of the best out of the professors you can take in the major in my opinion. I want anyone taking a class with him to be aware that you are NOT going to breeze through the class. He will make you work for it, but you will leave the class knowing much, much more than you did prior. The final is VERY cumulative, so study hard.

Deep Learning was a wild ride. Term project, weekly quizes, nightly video lectures (20 min-1hr), collaborative pop quizes basically every day, individual quizes each Friday. As I near graduation I realize that many of the courses which ended up having the largest impact on my education were all taught by Hutchinson (241, 367, ML, DL). Good stuff.
rating_tags: BEWARE OF POP QUIZZES--GROUP PROJECTS--Would take again

review_text: Taking 241 with Brian was my best experience at WWU. Before, I was intimidated and nervous for the course. But I truly enjoyed it and came out a lot more confident about CS. Weekly quizzes aren't too bad, especially since there was no midterm.  We had 3 projects throughout the quarter, each took 30-40 hours for me but were very fun.

Extremely good lecturer, before each lecture reviews topics, and shows you what topic is being covered during that class period. Has offices hours for at least 30 minutes every week day with a review session once a week. A great teacher who genuinely cares about his students learning.
The pair programming assignments were hard because of  scheduling but he was always flexible and available to help and his lectures were very well done.
rating_tags: ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--GRADED BY FEW THINGS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: Brian is the real deal. He's intelligent, quick on his feet, and communicates efficiently. He's a big believer in pair programming projects (uses SVN), weekly quizzes (fairly easy, study for like an hour the night before and you're fine), and ABCD cards. Never uses slides or a textbook (does suggest supplementary ones), no exams other than final.

For those of you looking to take Brian, consider this... His projects, and quizzes may not be scored in a manner that everyone agrees on, only because in CS, you can't afford to make some mistakes he docks you down on. He is always engaged with his students and very passionate when it comes to CS and teaching it. I highly recommend him.

He's a good professor, but he aims to be difficult. He shoots for a 70% average for quizzes, tests, and homeworks. The programming assignments are 50% of the grade, and they get graded in such a nit picky way that you might lose points for having an extra space in an output, or not having perfect formatting on a decimal number -- It's a bit absurd.

Took Machine Learning with Hutchinson.  Was a fairly difficult class but his examples really helped me catch up on the math behind the concepts.  Was difficult, but I learned a lot.  Group Projects were slightly annoying, and the final project was very annoying as throughout the class we learned how to implement things ourselves not use toolkits.

Brian is a really intelligent guy, and he definitely cares about his students. He teaches at a good pace and I thought he was pretty clear on everything. The one downside is the immense difficulty of projects and tests. He does do an "adjustment" for his tests that help. But nothing for the projects. Expect to give the projects a lot of time.

He aims for 70-80% on all quizzestestsassignments. If the class averages higher than that, he "adjusts" the following tests to include very minor details in the text. It ended up testing whether we were lucky enough to include certain facts in our notes (he allows one page per quiz) than if we actually learned the material well.
Very engaging lectures. Makes phenomenal use of ABCD cards and quick in class quizzes. Also just a great guy to hang out with.

Brian has been hands down the best professor I've had. He went out of his way to help everyone succeed to the point of scheduling out of class study sessions before exams. The projects were challenging and applied directly to the class content- you finished them feeling like you actually accomplished something.
Great clarity of work assigned. Had material completely internalized; never once taught out of a textbook.

Prof. Hutchinson is a very good prof, he is passionate about teaching and very intelligent. His programs were tough, but he graded fairly on exams and quizzes. I took 301 the first time he taught it and he was slightly scattered. Often his quizzes and exams ran long. 301 is a hard class in general, lots of information, but he teaches it well.

Brian is awesome! He goes at a slower pace and gives clear lectures, but he really expects you to know your stuff on exams and assignments. You have to work hard but he is really helpful and you will definitely learn a lot. One of my favorite professors of all time.

Great Professor! He challenges you and you have to work very hard, but you learn a lot. His projects/labs compliment the course material really well. The course material is difficult, but his lectures are very helpful. He is always happy to help with anything during office hours

One of my favorite CS professors so far. Projects are definitely challenging, but are interesting and fun to do. He can be a little knit-picky on grading, but as long as you put in the effort, a good grade will come out. Expect to learn a lot from taking his classes.

Great Professor, was readily available for help outside of class and did his best to explain things in terms you'd understand. Assignments were hard but very interesting, very language based but still very cool. Clear in class and gave thorough examples, very nice guy and great to work with. I learned a lot in this class and loved it. 10/10

Brian was an awesome teacher. The projects were a challenge, but at the same time doable if you didn't slack off. By the time I was done, I felt like way less of a noob at CS. I never even purchased the book because I never used it. The only thing I have to complain about is my grade. The grading was a little bit pickier than most.

Awesome teacher! Explains things very thoroughly and is very available for extra help. Just pay attention during lecture and you will keep up. Programs are fun and interesting, although a lot of people don't do too great on them. Wish i could take more classes from him. Great lecture pace. Definitely recommended.

Great prof. As long as you pay attention in class and don't daydream you should keep up. His labs tend to be almost just as hard as projects, but you get plenty of time to work on them. He is very devoted also. Very helpful in office hours, answers emails promptly and clearly, and he also stayed in the lab till very late hours to help people

Good guy, helpful outside of class and through emails.  Likes to follow the book, and use the books terminology (which can be confusing at times) and the labs are at times lengthy, but overall a good prof and class

Main problem is that he would go on tangents, then quickly mention something that is very important and on a quiz and go to the next topic before people have a chance to hear it or write it down..Other than that, he's a cool dude and nice instructor.

Shameem cares a lot about his students but this class was still not great. Lectures would be in depth but also could be very confusing. Exam dates were changed around several times making it very confusing and also would be graded quite harshly (one MCQ at 10 pts). Labs and projects are very difficult and harshly graded despite being correct.

Long, boring lectures with mandatory attendance. Would teach the basics, and then give projects and labs that were super complex. TA grading would say everything was great, and then still dock points for no reason. Advertised as a beginner class but not at all beginner friendly, don't take this class if you can avoid it.

As long as you come to class you will get a good grade. His lectures are pretty good, and make learning SQL pretty easy. Honestly did not like him when I took him for 145 but after having other professors, I have realized he's actually pretty good.
Super nice guy. Posts all of the lectures online so you can catch up and/or study easily. Lectures included lots of in class discussion relating to topics.

If you're looking to take a databases course I 100% recommend taking it with Ahmed. The lectures are useful, and the slides are some of the best I've seen in a CS course, they make studying for quizzes and exams so easy. The exams and quizzes are very fair, and as long as you study the slides, you'll do well (the midterm mean was a 90%).
Really good lectures and assignments made sense. Tests were somewhat difficult, but overall not excessively bad.

Very caring professor. He regularly sends out reminder emails for important deadlines and even granted me an extension for an important assignment I forgot to submit. He does his best to answer questions during and after class and tries to make sure everyone understands the topic before moving on.There's a lot to remember so get ready to study.
This class was good! The midterm was very short though.

Technology for Social Good was one of my favorite classes at WWU. I loved the structure of the class, and how the course was completely focused on project work and class participation. Projects are driven by student ideas, and group work is meaningful and compelling. Guest lectures were very informative and interesting.

It was his first quarter teaching 145. It was supposed to be an intro to Java and data structures but if you didn't already understand the topic, you will fail. I think he is just used to teaching higher level classes. Only rereads slides and is very uninvolved with the Labs, not even mentioning them once during lectures.

Really accommodating and understanding of his students. Would consistently check in with the class to make sure we were meeting deadlines, and pushing them out if needed. You can tell he cares for his students. I thought lectures were engaging too.

Taking him for CS145, no option for that though. Literally all he does is read word for word off of slides he does not teach at all. If you are at all a hands on learner like me do not take this class I have to go to separate professors just to understand the material. He also has a thick accent making it even harder to understand the slides.

His lectures are incredibly clear and succinct and he puts the slides online. He cares about each and every one of his students and he clearly has a passion for teaching. His tests are also clearly laid out; he makes practice questions for the exams with answers, notes, and tells you directly what will be on them. I cannot recommend him enough.
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class

review_text: Ahmed was a great professor. He very clearly cared about students understanding the concepts he was teaching and was very good about answering any questions we had. The only critique I'd say was that he went a bit fast through lectures at times, but he took our feedback on that and it was much better afterwards.

Ahmed is a good professor and very accommodating, but 412 is not a great course. If you want to get a job in Android Dev, just learn it yourself (better quality and free), but if you want a decent elective and easy A go for it. Only one test (the final), and almost all of the class is group projects.

Prof Ahmed handled online classes well and was a great professor. His slides were very useful and only tests based off what is on them. Quizzes/tests were almost perfect, I can only think of one question that was poorly worded. The only thing that needs to be improved is the clarity of his assignments instructions but you can just email him.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Test heavy

review_text: Extremely insensitive and not willing to help students in pandemic. Emailed us the course evaluations at 6:00 PM on Friday (the night they were due), so I was not able to give feedback. This is very unethical. Organizes class in a way that rewards cheating (by weighing online tests so heavily) and makes for an extremely unfair learning environment.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Graded by few things--Tough grader

review_text: Ahmed really does care about making sure you understand stuff. It's clear he's making an effort towards learning through group activities, which can be helpful. I found myself not having to go to lecture for most concepts as he posts slides that you can take a fair amount from. Assignments get a little difficult towards the end, but nothing crazy.

Dr. Ahmed is a very solid and capable professor. Databases is quite a boring topic, but Shameem's enthusiasm for the class and continued effort every lecture made the topic palatable. There is a bit of a language barrier, but after a couple classes it was no trouble understanding him. There is a lot to learn, so be sure to show up to class.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Caring

review_text: Solid professor that cares about student comprehension. Lectures are good and planned out ahead of time.  Very tough grader when it comes to tests/quizzes.

He seems like a nice guy, but 50% of class time revolved around using tiny powerpoint text to write queries. I told him early on that I couldn't read the text. After going to disability services to get accommodations, I got the slides and confirmed the text was on a image and about size 8. Decided to drop after wasting 5 weeks squinting at board.

Good powerpoints, and I liked his emphasis on in-class practice and participation. Made sure everyone understood concepts before moving on. The first project was easy but the second two were a lot more difficult. Lenient grader on tests. Weekly quizzes with no surprises- expectations were clear. Overall a good professor

Great professor. Had an excellent time taking this class. His accent is a bit thick and can sometimes be difficult to understand when he speaks quietly, so beware if you are not that great at understand accents. He is very thorough and wants to make sure the entire class understands a concept before continuing on. He has great powerpoints as well
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS

review_text: Ahmed is not a great lecturer but he really cares about student comprehension so don't be afraid to ask questions when you're confused. Some test questions tended to be a bit ambiguous and the projects didn't give great database experience.
Work hard and make an effort and he will appreciate it.

Lecture slides read verbatim and lifted from the textbook. Never understood student's understanding given he never graded or looked at a single question a student turned in. Passed the attendance sheet around during a midterm to make sure people would sign it. Posted names of Students who performed well on the midterm in an attempt to "motivate".

Ahmed hasn't contributed anything to this course outside of standing in front of class and giving abysmally dry lectures. If you get the stock projects again, get ready to learn nothing about databases and all about parsing stuff in Java! Oh, and your grade hinges on the TAs ability to grade fairly (and accurately) because Ahmed doesn't grade.

This class had potential to be great, but was terrible. No structure whatsoever. The entire course was just reading, reviewing and writing papers, and presenting the same powerpoint over and over again. Final project was senior-project complexity, self-taught with only 3 weeks to complete. Took attendance to get people to show up. Do not take!!

Incredibly frustrating class. Half your grade is exams that are confusingly worded and under extreme time pressure. Exam average is usually a D AFTER the curve. Our 3rd exam average was 20 percent. The professor is nice but makes this class way harder than it should be.

Her homework, exercises, labs are written by another teacher. Her "lectures", she runs out of time attempting the program(you just turned in) herself instead of having a solution already to show. And she has no solutions to any of the programs or tests The tests are bugged BEWARE: the tests are badly written, you will not know what is being asked
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: Dr Hardin Was not my professor  The textbook, and Scott Wherwein (who made all the course content despite Hardin actually being the professor) were my professors.  Attendance is mandatory despite lectures not teaching any course content.  Avoid this professor at all costs, I got a good grade in this class and even I thought it was miserable

maybe im just dense. but i went to office hours, i went to tutoring, i did outside work on w3 schools and youtube, but i'm still going to get a bad grade because the exams are formatted horrendously, and they're worth 50% of your grade. if you wanna end the quarter with your ego still intact choose someone else. TA's were lovely though !

The class is doable if you put in the time, but nearly all of your learning is done via videos outside of class. She doesn't spend too much class time at all on teaching. I would've skipped the lectures if our attendance wasn't marked.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Her lectures are decent and understandable, but if you ask questions she might not answer them clearly (which is kind of the nature of the class). Overall fine, if you study up you can pass the exams and get 100% on the homeworks/labs. If you don't understand what's going on in lecture, go to the TA or your friends, not her OH.

You learn the material at home through videos, do review in class. I hate this style of class, but what can you do? She's nice, but doesn't really teach. Basically your whole grade is 2 exams. Best advice: go to labs and ask your TA for help there, not prof. Hardin-- it's a huge class, so her office hours are always busy.
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Not the class for beginner programmers or those with DAC accommodations. Doesn't really teach anything herself, using other profs lecture videos for all new content and barely goes over answers to exceedingly difficult homework in class. Nice but the only people who were really successful from what I saw were those who already knew a lot about CS.

She is a very kind professor, but relies heavily on textbook/recorded lectures from other professors to teach the class and barely teaches herself. Alright for students who know Python, but it's horrible if you are a beginner to computer sci. Additionally, her assignments/exams are always full of bugs and errors, and she is not very understanding.

Dr. Hardin is a very nice teacher with good lectures and provides a good amount of office hours. Having taken her class of this year, we were the guinea pig group for a different exam style. During many of them we were interrupted by TA's to fix errors in the script NOT made by us. It became irritating in addition to being in a time restriction.

Dr. Hardin is a nice person who cares about her students, but I don't think she puts in enough preparation time into her teaching. Errors on her examples and daily exercises were one thing, but there were also a lot of errors on quizzes and tests. This would be a very difficult class to take if you didn't already mostly know the material.

I absolutely would not recommend taking Caroline for SCED 480 and 490. She is unorganized, unhelpful, and appears to not care about the class understanding scientific concepts. Her grading is often late and she leaves irrelevant feedback. I left this class without any additional knowledge on how to teach science in the elementary classroom.

I attended office hours and time outside of office hours, only for her to tell me that she had a meeting she forgot about.  I previously mentioned that I learned differently than other students and needed extra help from her and possibly other tutors.  Just to be later called out wrongfully for being academically dishonest.

Dr. Hardin was a very fun professor and her teaching style worked very well for me. I had previous programming experience so the content was easy to digest for me, but I know some completely new to programming people who struggled with her teaching. She was also rather disorganized and forgot to post things sometimes, or posted the wrong things.

Class was a lot harder than it should have been, largely due to the professor and her teaching style/lack of self awareness. She was rude to students and condescending as a person. I don't know how she is still a teacher but she was barely present to teach or do anything other than make life difficult for students.

Honestly a terrible ASSISTANT professor. She is incredibly rude and entitled. Her time is leagues more precious to her than your own so don't bother with emails or trying to talk, she won't reply. Her office hours are useless as is her teaching. The TAs were better teachers and taught more often than she did anyways. What a waste of tuition money.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: I would not recommend SCED 480 with Caroline. Her class lacked organization and the lectures were very scattered and hard to follow. Unreasonable attendance policy, in which zooming into class gave you 85% (even when sick) and missing class was 0% with no way to make up points. Weekly homework load was fine, but feel my learning was insufficient.

This class was made out to be a very easy class, but it isn't. The Professor wasn't often available and lectures were hard to understand because she doesn't explain properly.The topics were always too difficult to understand and the prof doesn't explain much in detail. The prof also discriminates you if your grades are bad and use it against you.

Prof wasn't available a lot of the time and lectures were hard to understand because she went over concepts way too fast. Quizzes/exams were fairly easy but the assignments were almost impossible towards the end of the quarter. Prof also took months to grade assignments and messed up the grades for the class multiple times. Disorganized overall.

The majority of the grade for this class is based off of coding projects, the midterm and final are each only worth like 10% of your grade which is nice. She always has a zoom version of the class, so if you're not able to make the in person lecture, you can join over zoom. She sometimes forgets to post assignments on time, but otherwise is great.

Dr. Hardin cares about the material and her students but does fall short of being as clear and concise as one needs to be when teaching an introductory class to students who might never have opened up an IDE in their lives. If you are new to compsci it's completely possible to get an A in this class, but don't underestimate your TA's!

This class is not easy if you don’t put in the necessary effort. You will have to put in extra time outside of class to succeed. Expect spend about 6-15 hours on assignments since are hard. However there’s only about 5 assignments and 8 labs. The labs are very easy compared to the homework. If you don’t do well teaching yourself don’t take.

The professor is an understanding person, but this class feels like it was set up for people who have programmed before. I went to a tutor for one of my CSCI assignments and even he found it difficult. You have to figure it out by yourself when it comes to actually doing assignments. My TA never read the lab before the lab so no help there either.
Great professor. You will learn a lot and the class is fun, but also a lot of work.

141 was a great introduction to Python and Dr. Hardin taught a great class. Grading criteria were clear, the exams were de-prioritized (10% for both midterm and final), and the cutoff for an A was 90%. You definitely have an opportunity in Hardin's class to get an A, even if you're COMPLETELY new to programing like I was in this course.

she was super understanding and friendly. i dont usually review teachers on this site but i wanted to leave her a nice review because she was so lovely. she does make the occasional mistake or two, but it's nothing major. class get really hard if you fall behind on work, but that's expected of a CS class. she clearly cares about her students.

Such an awesome professor. Passionate about her work, kind, understanding, and funny. Super bummed to be taking her class over zoom because I'd love to get to know her more and learn some more of her wisdom!

Hardin is new at the university and just needs more time to fall in. She has trouble conveying info in lectures in an organized and easy-to-follow manner but overall very willing to work with her students. If you're new to CS this class will be challenging but rewarding. Coding projects can be long; I recommend finding a whiteboard!

She has a ton of of csci smarts & is willing to work with her students. 2 assignments a week (lab and hw) with 1 in class quiz on random day of week. The assignments and labs will take lots of time if you're a novice in programming. There are resources to help you but self learning seems to be extremely promoted. If you have exp, its an easy class.

Tarek is a nice person. However, I strongly disliked this class. His lectures are cumbersome and the slides are often unclear on the material. He is frustrating to have a class with because his expectations are so confusing, and change so often. Specific requirements were often not clarified until right before the deadline of an assignment.

Operating systems class is one of the hardest at Western in CS, Prof Idriss is pretty decent at teaching this class and the lecture content was understandable for the most part. Assignments were hard but that is on-par for the class; however, Prof Idriss had very little office hours (1.5 hours a week).

Takes attendance sometimes and has lecture exercises to turn in that are gone over in class. Assignments can be time consuming sometimes but not impossible if you start early. Tests/exams were extremely fair and covered the material shown on the slides, which are thorough enough that I did not need to look at the textbook at all for this class.
rating_tags: Amazing lectures --Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: The lectures can be a little fast paced, but overall they were easy to understand if you pay attention and go to class. He was very reasonable and gave opportunities for extra credit and curved grades. The assignments can be pretty difficult, one in particular was extremely time consuming and many people couldn't finish it. Overall, good professor.

Tarek was a good professor. Helpful office hours. Lectures were informative but fast paced. Assignments are not too difficult - aside from 1 that is very hard. (Bumps difficulty from 3->4!) They sort of give step-by-step solutions. If you read ahead in the textbook you'll read a lot of things we don't go over in class.

Seems like a nice guy, no ill will towards him, but he has absolutely zero regard for students time.  I mean this class is equivalent to 2 normal classes in terms of workload. In the next 6 days I need to do 7 AWS labs and 2 quizzes in addition to like 2 hours of lecture videos, in addition to attending the class lectures.  Not cool.

He plays a game gotcha with critical info that he chooses to give out unannounced. This information has nothing to do with actual content of the class but will severely effect your grade (such as critical exam details purposely not included in other communication).  We paid for his disorganization (losing 10min of exam time because of his error)

Tarek's lectures are dense and his slides are needlessly complex. He was hopelessly behind on grading for our class and relied on the TA for essentially all of it. He drops quizzes when the class does badly and curves, which was nice, but he doesn't really have a passion for teaching.  I'll avoid taking him again, but you could do worse.

Lectures disorganized and DENSE. Most of the actual content is annotations that are not made available to review. Idriss did not seem to care if students had an understanding of the content, instead dropping tests when most of the class failed and never circling back. Very little student intuition, the class had to ask him to ease up often.

The only 47s class I enjoyed. It might just be compared to the other instructors, but Tarek has engaging lectures, along with worksheets to help understanding. Labs and homeworks can be difficult, but there's plenty of time to finish and ask questions. Solid professor, especially compared to the 47s teachers.

Tarek is always a solid choice. He does go a bit fast in lectures, so sometimes it's hard to keep up. He was really accommodating and helpful in office hours, and his tests were challenging but not impossible. I had him for cybersecurity a while back, and he has improved his teaching method a lot since then.

Tarek is one of my favorite professors at WWU. The instructions on all assignments weren't particularly clear but if you asked him about it he would provide more than enough clarity to proceed. The rootkit project is most unique assignment I've ever had. Most of the projects are really cool as they involve you get to hack into a virtual network.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Accessible outside class--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: He explains topics very well making lectures easy to understand and is helpful when you ask questions. There are daily worksheets you turn in based on a couple questions in lecture that everyone answers together. 447 can be a difficult class for some but I found that Professor Idriss does a great job of making difficult concepts simple

Computer security with him was a pain. The instructions for the assignments weren't the clearest, and his responses to email questions were unhelpful. He seemed very standoffish and doesn't take student criticism well. Had this been an in person class, perhaps it would have been different. I'm going to try to not take classes with him going forward
rating_tags: Participation matters--Skip class? You won't pass.--Graded by few things

review_text: Everyday, we were given an online worksheet to complete as we follow along in class. He encouraged participation, even if you are unsure of the answer. About every two weeks there is some sort of quiz. Had labs and homework assignments, and they mostly are regarding the Blitz system. If you fall behind, send a message to him, and he is great on 1:1
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Clear grading criteria--Amazing lectures

review_text: Professor Eglaly is absolutely amazing. 347 is a super hard course and will take a lot of your effort, but she makes it a lot more managable and wantsn you to succeed. Her quizzes are pretty tough, but once I figured out how to study for them it got better. No tests, only projects and quizzess makes the class feel like you learn a lot about coding.

I found the course to be difficult, but Elglaly is accommodating and I found her policies very fair. No exams but difficult weekly quizzes. Projects are hard and long but very applicable to the material and great for learning. She is clearly caring for her students and I found her presence very enjoyable. Would recommend and take her again.
She is very kind and accommodating. Take her class if you can.

347 is difficult with most other professors, Yasmine isn't as difficult but it never felt like I wasn't learning as much as the other sections. No exams, just quizzes, exercises, and projects. Guidance for the projects isn't really there, but she gives time in-class to work with her. Go to office hours, email doesn't work to get her attention well.

Some of the professors in CSCI are very challenging. Yasmine is easier than some (not a free A). When I took this class it had no exam. She responded to emails and was a caring person. The only thing that was frustrating was the activities. A lot of the grade is 5-point activities, so if the grader takes off 1 point, you got an 80% on it!

Very caring and kind professor. Lectures are assigned as homework and worksheets are done in small groups in-class, which worked fairly well for me personally. Homework assignments are done in pairs. Assignment directions are occasionally confusing.

No tests, just projects. Elglaly is the best professor I have ever had. She cares immensely about her students and is extremely emotionally intelligent, always picking up where her class is at. I cannot recommend her more. 347 was a lot of fun!
Professor Elglaly is always extremely cheerful. She cares about her students and likes to see them succeed. She is one of the most helpful instructors I've had.

Friendly and helpful when asked, reasonable work load assigned, very helpful in class when someone had a question. If you need help she is always the best person to ask if you're working on something.
She doesn't teach the class, as you just watch youtube videos and then fill out worksheets. Weekly quizzes and assignments were honestly kind of rough.

Professor was really friendly and accommodating to students. It was nice to have small assignments before jumping into a larger assignment. I really enjoyed having the exams be actual programming activities. The overall pacing was great.

The lectures were very detailed and covered a lot of information and examples. The assignments and projects were very relevant to what we learned and I enjoyed doing them. The way the midterm and the final was formatted made me feel relaxed and didn't give anxiety while taking them.
The professor explained most topics extremely well, was helpful when asked clarifying questions and was able to explain complex topics in multiple ways. A lot of hands-on activities

The obvious pitfall of hybrid classes aside, I think anyone who shows up to class or reads the textbook will pass with flying colors. Tests were timed programming assignments. Projects are graded harshly, but its all on the rubric. She extended project deadlines when needed. Lectures were horrible though, basically just CTRL+V the C manual in Linux

Well-intentioned, but a little bit of a hot mess. ie class always starts late, lectures aren't very organized, and assignment directions aren't clear. She does look for feedback from her students, wants everyone to do well, and is relatively understanding.
What an amazing guy!

Grades assignments super late, leaving students worried at the end of the quarter about their final grade. Extremely strict grader on quizzes and exams, especially on subjective questions he barely gives any partial credit. Does not know how to divide up project workload properly between weeks, giving massive lists of tasks for the final few weeks.

Shri seems to not care about the students in his class. He fails to grade in a reasonable amount of time, making it near impossible to correct holes in your understanding of the material. You're grade will suffer because of this. Quizzes have questions with multiple correct answers, only one will be accepted. Nightmare class 0/10.

I think Shri is getting a bad rap due to this class's difficulty. I don't feel that exam questions were unfairly written, but they are different from what we worked with class (synthesis over basic understanding). Homework is dense, but helpful as practical a example. Lectures are informative and well paced. Exams and quizzes were a bit rushed.

Weekly quizzes, 9 total. 5 Coding Assignments, 3 Exams. The coding assignments are extremely long, with the assignment descriptions being around 30 pages. Homework assignments can take 20+ hours. The exams are completely different from homework. He was usually late to class by 5 minutes, and would always rush lectures despite being late.

This was the worst class I've ever taken, the lectures are the only saving grace of this class. the quizzes and exams were the most horribly designed I have ever seen, exams were not based on what we were told they would be on top of including material not taught in class, and assignments are from Toyos. The documentation is horrific and unclear.

- Frequently late to lectures - Lectures felt super rushed - Weekly quizzes felt super rushed - Lecture content was cool but did NOT help much for the toyos project - Toyos documentation was less fun than reading a dictionary - Exams were also super rushed 447 should really be 2 classes, not 1 (or more time should be given for exams).

- Poorly designed homework (uses ToyOS) - Unrealistic quizzes (10 minute quizzes with 20 mins of work) - Gives bad advice on homework if they respond at all - Most poorly structured exams I have ever seen - Ignores student feedback - Will lie to you about what's on an exam, class avg: 40% there is a lot more i could say about this dumpster fire

Shri was a great professor. 447 is one of the hardest classes in the major and you could tell that Shri was doing his best to make sure students understood the content. He was extremely helpful in office hours and gave tons of opportunity to do extra credit if you were struggling. Overall a great professor, even if the classes are somewhat hard.

Great guy. Super caring + helpful and assignments were very fair. Tests were kind of tricky (lots of questions and not a ton of time) but if you studied a lot for them and took notes they were totally doable, no trick questions/stuff that wasn't covered. You learn a good bit in his classes without it ever being too hard, I enjoyed them.

Shri went above and beyond to provide a good learning environment remotely for spring 21. He was available almost any hour of the day, even past midnight. Hes very generous with the no-penalty late days and provides really good feedback. Im already registered for another class with him in the fall.

CS 367 is known to be a very difficult class, taking it with Shri was a great choice. He is very caring and makes himself available on discord all the time. The only reason I gave him a 4 and not a 5 is because the lectures could be a bit fast paced and very dense, I'm not sure if this is due to being online or just the course though.

Amazing professor. One of the kindest, most caring, and most accessible professors I've ever had, which is a rarity in CS. Quick to respond, and he would take time outside of both class and office hours (sometimes in the evening) if needed to help students. The projects are large but are doable if you take your time and ask for help when needed.
Really good during office hours, subject material was covered exceedingly well and thorough. graduated this quarter, but if I didn't, I would have tried to take more classes with him

Lectures were quality and engaging. Office hours are very valuable and helpful. Homeworks were very difficult but graded forgivingly. Kam leans a lot on his math background which can be distracting and can sometimes make a simple concept very confusing. Brush up on proofs before taking this class of 405.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Lots of homework--Accessible outside class

review_text: The class is not easy by any means, but Harris did a great job explaining difficult concepts and algorithms. The algorithms covered are directly applicable to programming interviews, especially FAANG companies. He is kind and very willing to help, so do not hesitate to ask questions if you are struggling!

The topics that are covered are meant to be difficult and stretch core and advanced topics, Kameron does an amazing job teaching the proper approaches. HW is worth most of your grade, and the amount of it reflects that. If you understand all HW and quizzes, even if you did not get them all correct, the final will be straight forward!

Dr. Harris is a kind professor whose quizzes and exams are usually modified versions of in-class assignments. His 305 is very math-heavy since his specialization is in applied mathematics. You'll often be one hour into a lecture when he suddenly states that, "none of this will be on the test." Overall, it's an average class with a decent professor.
rating_tags: Test heavy--Graded by few things--Accessible outside class

review_text: Dr. Harris is such an amazing professor. He goes out of his way to work with and get to know students and does a lot to guide you on a difficult topic. Ask questions and go to his office hours and he'll clear things up, though extensions on assignments are rare. Grade weight is very heavy on homework then pretty light for quizzes and participation.

Difficult class near the beginning, the homework required a lot of outside research and reading from the lectures. Answered questions very well and was sympathetic when it came to grading / quizzes. One of the best professors I've taken so far at Western.

I started the quarter off on a bad foot, Kameron emailed me and helped me dissect what I have misunderstood and gave me opportunities to boost my grade and help myself. He truly cares for his students and made that evident in my case. He motivated me into going to office hours, something I have been scared with since starting college during covid.

Kameron will go above and beyond. Multiple extra office hours when needed. Great at answering questions and working with students to find the solution. Didn't need the textbook since his lectures are much more meaningful. Very tough grader, but gives the option to revise HW. He's great at showing application examples. Hard class, would take again.

Harris has been pretty great this quarter. He's a nice guy and was incredibly easy to talk to whenever I needed help with something. We didn't have any exams and were graded primarily on 5 homework assignments, which we were allowed to work together on, and this made the class far less stressful, which was AWESOME. Recommended.
rating_tags: Accessible outside class--Graded by few things--EXTRA CREDIT

review_text: For his first time teaching, he did great. The structure of the class could have been better, but the knowledge and care he put into teaching was above and beyond. Very nice as well. The class was very math heavy but for a machine learning course that should be expected. He thoroughly challenges you but you come out better. Highly recommend him!

Professor Harris is great, he knows the topic well, is always ready to provide assistance and cares about the students. However, there is a level of math understanding that is crucial to passing this class, and if you don't understand the lingo then this class will be very difficult. Since math is the tool that is used to describe the ML topics.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Accessible outside class--Skip class? You won't pass.

review_text: Kameron's first quarter at WWU. He's nice, but he is disconnected from his student's ability. Course was taught super math heavy. Everything is based off of 224 and 304, which he doesn't teach you the concepts of. No tests which is nice, and assignments are usually with groups, but still leave you lost. Could definitely see him improving though
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Group projects--Accessible outside class

review_text: The subject matter was not bad, but the professor seems to talk about nothing the whole time. Tests and assignments are oddly specific for only having high-level concepts covered in class. The most teaching that was done was the creation of gotcha problems. No textbook (only recommended) so you have to rely on endless powerpoint slides and lecture.

Course was way too easy. I have a friend who took it from another prof. We covered nothing compared to what they learned. If you want to do virtually nothing and get an A or B for it this is the section for you. Wish I had taken it from someone else so I could have actually learned something.

Very good prof. He's really good at explaining the material and he's super chill and approachable. His assignment descriptions could be more thorough, as half the battle is figuring out what you're supposed to do, but the assignments themselves are very straightforward. Definitely reach out if you're struggling. Wish I did that more haha.
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Group projects--Amazing lectures

review_text: Michael is a great professor! Would definitely take this class with him again!

Michael is really cool and caring guy, however I disliked this course as a computer science class. You have 6 projects, only one of which involves any code. The others consist of very basic sql commands as well as writing reports and creating diagrams. Stressful online quizzes give you 1 min/ question, and each question is about 1% of your grade
rating_tags: Graded by few things--Group projects--Lecture heavy

review_text: I would highly recommend attending class unless you have a strong background in databases. The first couple assignments had auto graders so there was no reason to not get 100%. Quiz and final were online with short time frames but relatively easy. Definitely loved the lectures, very informative professor.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--Skip class? You won't pass.--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Great great great. Extremely knowledgeable, helpful and understanding. Truly one of the best. The projects can be difficult, start early!
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--GROUP PROJECTS

review_text: First quarter at WWU and Michael is probably one of my favorite professors ever. Super great and always available for help. Networks is still difficult, so be prepared for quizzes and read the textbook. The two projects were challenging but doable and had a group presentation project at the end.
rating_tags: Gives good feedback--ACCESSIBLE OUTSIDE CLASS--Clear grading criteria

review_text: Professor Ural was very nice but his assignments were very time consuming for an intro language but were nice when finally done. Midterms and final weren't as much difficult as they were just tricky. Easy to just miss little things and get low score. Averages were too low for what they should be but honestly the tests weren't hard.
Really difficult tests.  65% class average.  TA was a life saver.  Only slightly helpful, during office hours.  Interesting class though.

Like most people said, his assignments are a pain and VERY time consuming. When finished, they are pretty cool, and you do learn a lot. I would recommend taking him, but make sure your other classes are easy!

I expected to learn a lot about high level animation (in Maya) and/or low level animation (working with animated models with code).  Instead, we covered some Maya basics and then spent the last half of the quarter covering math (splines and graphics projections) and never really got to any real animation.  I recommend you avoid any class with him.

Don't take his classes if you're looking for a good grade...you'll spend the entire quarter sure you're going to fail, and then you will. He doesn't curve grades and is perfectly content to fail the entire class. His tests are lethal. Having said that though...you'll learn more in his class than any other. Highly recommended if you can handle an F
It's the nicest little man ever, but his assignments are freaking scary.  He's one smart dude though

The class is time consuming, but you will walk out with some of the coolest programs you have ever built. Another plus is that he does not give any tests, so grades entirely on programming assignments.

Amazing, easy-to-follow lectures with notes posted online. The homework is very doable, and it's easy to ask questions and get helpful answers. Expectations are extremely clear, and exams closely match lecture content and homework. He also gave 5% extra credit for attendance and participation, verified with work from lecture questions.
Very easy class

Amr is a well rounded professor who seems to genuinely care about his students. Grading criteria was unclear at times, and it seemed impossible to get 100% in anything he graded. There was always something missing from anything you submitted, and there often wasn't any explanation of what should be improved. Besides that he was a great professor.

Amr has been one of my favorite EECE professors so far. He always teaches in a very straightforward manner. The material that is on the exams is the same as what we did in the homework, which is the same as what we learned in class. Rarely any curveball or extrapolated questions. He seems to genuinely care about his student's success and learning.

Actually got me interested in paying attention and learning things well. He had difficultish homework assignments, but the test content was never surprising and often less difficult than the hw. Standup guy, would take again.

Amr genuinely cares about his student's success. He makes himself available to ask questions, and always can provide useful feedback and explanations to problems that don't make sense. Always quick to respond to canvas messages and answers questions thuroughly. His exams are always straightforwards and cover the material we learned in class.
He does a good job of explaining things, and can usually offer alternative explanations if something doesn't make sense.

Prof does not teach or care about his students. Shows no respect. Does not answer questions but instead his tone is as if you were under him for asking the question. Would not recommend at all. Prof should reflect on his ability to teach and influence his students.
hes the reason why WPI has high mental health issues.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: taking this class is like telling a horse to lick its elbow, impossible. he doesn't teach in class and the modulus are all on tryhackme which take so much of my time while juggling other classes. his grading system is a new kind that doesn't work at all and stressed me out. all the questions that are asked in the discord are about grading, not work

Grading is done on a separate scale that is all-or-nothing in nature. Most valuable material is taught through tryhackme and not by the professor himself. Exams are auto-fail for a letter grade if one question in that letter grade's category is missed. These are made up in office hours. Prof only has 2hr/wk OH and the final has no OH. Good luck.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Test heavy--Graded by few things

review_text: Professor Walls does not teach. He just has a class discussion for the entirety of every class about cyber security related events. He then assigns an unreasonable amount of homework from an online website about things not discussed in class. Everything I have learned is self taught from the website and not from him.

So completely out of touch with the students and the amount of homework he gives. Unreasonable expectations for homework and tests. Homework consistently takes double to triple the time he says it will and requires information he doesn't teach or mention. He hasn't written a single line of code during lecture despite it being a CS course.

Despite no security prereqs the class is literally only run through a CTF website. CTF is made for people who already know the exploit playbook and rewards grit over comprehension. There is no textbook and many HWs rely on info poorly communicated in class. It's not a matter of difficult content. The format is challenge based not educational.
Professor Walls was one of the most engaging and fun professors I had at WPI
rating_tags: Clear grading criteria--Gives good feedback--Lots of homework

review_text: THIS PROFESSOR ABSOLUTELY BLOWS. He has zero social awareness and does not relate to students. Okay lectures and his jokes dont land.
is very condescending
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Lots of homework

review_text: OS is one of the harder classes at WPI, and Walls doesn't really attempt to make his students' experience in the class much better. Not very open to helping outside of class, and gives unclear advice on IA when help is needed. Also needed to republish test cases for multiple HWs because of some error, which hurt students who were already stressing

I had a disappointing experience with Professor Robert Walls as my OS professor. I found that he was not very helpful and seemed to lack interest in his students' success. Whenever I asked him questions, he would either give me short, unhelpful answers or seemed dismissive of my concerns.
Very tough grader. This guy does not care about his students.

Would frequently cut students off, made the quizzes super difficult and essentially wanted us to fail. He also had no interest in responding back to students on time or helping them out, as he only had one office hour per week, and they would switch up every week.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Graded by few things

review_text: Sucks
I struggled so much in this class it genuinely made me want to reconsider my major or even drop out. Professor walls does not care about his students or their mental health at all
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--So many papers

review_text: Tough class with brutal projects, and he made it tougher. Quizzes were tricky, and Walls never shoots you straight. It's almost like he's constantly trying to trick you, and it's not a welcoming learning environment.  Every 1-on-1 encounter I had with him, he seemed annoyed and dismissive of my questions. Future students: beware, and good luck.

I thought this course was in C# before I registered so I thought I could coast and load up elsewhere with hard classes and when it ended up being in C but C is so much harder. I really struggled to keep up with this course.

Beware! Professor Robert's OS class is like a horror movie. You'll be scared, confused, and begging for it to end. I give him a solid 0.5 out of 10, and that's only because I'm feeling charitable. So, if you're looking for a good time, stay far away from his class. But if you're into self-punishment, go ahead - just make sure to bring aspirin

Very strict grader and not very understanding. Robert puts numerous truck questions on the exams and assigns very heavy amounts of homework. Very difficult to understand lectures and frequently mumbles to himself.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--So many papers

review_text: I do not like this Professor.

Okay lectures, kinda funny guy. This class was hard and learned a lot but would have liked to see more understanding towards students and adjustment to course to better cater towards a learning environment, especially more accessible office hours and showing respect to students.
Bad professor
rating_tags: Tough grader--Lots of homework--Beware of pop quizzes

review_text: This class was a mess. Quizzes were structured for you to fail. They were closed book, in class quizzes. The questions were very specific and they were graded very harshly. On top of that the quizzes were retroactive, so anything you have learned in the class, can be on the quiz. Concepts are dense. Please take with another professor.
good teacher. funny lectures. strict grader and tough quizzes but you learn a lot from his class.
rating_tags: Tough grader--Get ready to read--Group projects

review_text: I spent more than 20+ hours on each project per week, so his class was pretty stressful for me. However, if you plan on earning only a passing grade, then his class is pretty doable. No tests/quizzes/exams, but you will have projects every week, which will count as majority of your grade.

I went into this class hearing that it was a difficult one, however, I was pleasantly surprised by how organized and assisting Prof. Walls was. This class is a very technical topic of OS but you really learn a lot. The lectures are really useful and the projects are actually fun to do. It's rare to have a project , or even class, be so engaging.

Very good class. Concentrated on low-level security. Instead of forcing you to read challenging research papers, he presented them as concepts to the class during lecture, which I really liked. Very clear with his explanations of concepts. Like most (if not all) of his classes, the grading was 45% projects, 45% Midterm/Final, 10% participation.
(DS1010) Super easy class, all homeworks and quizzes done outside of class as the only work graded. All work followed lecture material, mainly read off slidedeck for every lecture.

The course is graded on short, remote quizzes and weekly assignments. The quizzes reflect the lectures/powerpoints. Expect to learn a lot outside of class and go to every office hour to get the assignments done. None of the assignments or quizzes have deadlines except that you submit them by the last day of the term, which is helpful.
This was my first class in masters and professor Xiangnan Kong is a real gem. We learnt a lot from him. Great teaching and a very nice person.

Prof. Kong is one of the great professor in WPI. Even though his example is simple, his homework are considerably quite challenging but not so challenging that it is impossible to do. He is very knowledgeable on his expertise and if you have any question, he will answer it right away. Prepare for a lot of movie memes and funny video.

Professor Kong is by far the worst professor I've had at WPI. He's very friendly and willing to work with students, but that fact that this course is a 4000 level CS class is a joke. I learned very little. I stopped going to class and easily got an A.

Kong is one of the most respectful Professors for me in WPI. He has comprehensive knowledge of machine learning and always able to answer tough questions intuitively. The homework is a design by himself with an amazing structure and test cases. The only bad thing is he focuses on storytelling too much and may jump hard concepts so quickly.

Professor Kong is a friendly professor, but he's the worst lecturer I ever had at WPI. I was unable to follow most of the lectures, even on toics I already knew about. He tried to explain everything TOO clearly (which made the lectures impossible to follow), but still leaves out important details on how everything fits together.

The class had virtually no overlap with the course description. Course content was either full of irrelevant material (e.g. a video on Facebook corporate culture, an Apple Face ID ad, a clip from the film "Moneyball", etc.), poorly explained with too many simplistic examples, or so niche as to be useless. I stopped showing up and still got an A.

You'll pass easily, but you'll learn nothing unless you put in the time to teach yourself, which is necessary for neither the homeworks nor the quizzes. In some cases, the homework doesn't help at all topic, because it's so specific to one part of the topic. This is the class that made me realize I'd prefer a hard class to a bad one.
Caring and always ready to help students. The course can be challenging but he explains the concepts very clearly if you make the effort to try.

Fantastic lectures for learning computer networks. He also marks his slides so you know exactly what to study for the quiz and has sample midterm and finals that correlate very well. Projects are well-structured in a way that helps you understand further about the materials.  Goes above and beyond for students. Genuine and caring. Highly recommend

His lecture is a real gem. He motivates people with his lecture and clearly shows how the current research (well, not so current but within 5 years range) are going. Projects are really helpful for you to understand the concept. Yes, it is hard and required a lot of resources but it is provided. It is worth your time at night to learn this class!

A remarkable and helpful professor, organized contents of the course very well and the concepts were clearly explained. It was an enjoyment to take this class as its logical hierarchy made me very comfortable. And he always expressed his willingness to help students and can always explain the concepts and questions in an easily understandable way.
he is a good teacher.

Li is a good professor. He want students to learn something new rather than finishing homework and exam. If you can prove you tried your best and have good attitude, he will grade u A. Highly recommend his DS 504. It is a paper based lecture and you can learn a lot from it.

Very good lecturer teaching on a super interesting topic. Exams, quizzes, and labs are all fair and generally easy. Programming projects are a lot of work but there are plenty of office hours to help, and they are pretty fun projects once you get the hang of them. You don't need to go to his lectures to do well but I highly recommend going anyway.
rating_tags: Lots of homework--Accessible outside class--Amazing lectures

review_text: I literally never go to any of my CS lectures because I do better self-studying, but Li's lecture's are worth waking up in the morning for. Super helpful and it is very clear what the quizzes and exams are on so you know how to prepare.

He is a pretty good professor. Gives plenty of chances for extra credit, even though you shouldn't need to do it to get an A. Two exams, 8-10 quizzes (lowest one being dropped, plus you can use a cheat sheet on each), 3 individual programming projects, and 3 easy lab assignments. Willing to modify deadlines/ assignments if necessary.

This class was a waste of my time. The lectures were completely unstructured and all we learned to do was draw and animate things. I can see the appeal for un-grading for those who are less driven to learn, or get anxiety from grades, but I really felt like the whole class was more about the reflections than actually learning code in useful ways.

Professor Smith and her ungraded course made me learned so much more compared to normal graded classes. She is the go-to professor if you want to learn a lot of knowledge while also not getting stressed in the term.

Professor Smith's class was ungraded, so we told her what grade we believed we earned at the end. That immediately reduced any stress about programming! Her follow-along lectures were worth attending, and the term-long group project was the best learning opportunity. The lab and reading assignments were not nearly as useful in my opinion.
rating_tags: Get ready to read--Group projects--Online Savvy

review_text: Phenomenal professor! She took a completely ungraded approach to programming which I really enjoyed! This approach not only made it easy and accessible to learn how to program, but encouraged learning from my mistakes in my learning. Also no exams or quizzes! Instead we had to reflect on our ability to program! I wish I could take her again for CS!
A great professor. I'm still using what she taught after the term is over. She covers very interesting topic in class. You won't regret if you decide to take class with her.

I've worked with Professor Smith on several occasions now and each one has been a delightful experience. Whenever I had a problem both academically or externally, she was there to assist and advise me, going the extra steps to set up ISP's to help me pursue my dreams. Her teaching methods are slightly unconventional, but certainly in a good way.

Lane is an awesome guy and a great professor, would definately recommend taking Algos with him if he teaches it. Very down to earth and caring about his students, also attentive to feedback including coding in lecture to help students understand the algorithms presented.

Algorithms is one of the driest CS courses out there, but Professor Harrison was able to make it fun and interesting by finding ways to insert humor and relatable topics. He's extremely easy to get in contact with and clearly understands how to use and troubleshoot the tools he gives.

He's really knowledgeable and keeps up to date on data vis/other data science research. Very easy to talk to and a great resource if you're looking for some real professional insight into the industry. Class had a reasonable difficulty level. I felt like I learned a lot about data vis that wasn't just "here's Tableau and a data set, go ham"
I really enjoyed data visualization - Professor Harrison taught it really well. The early projects were a bit simplistic, but built up to very interesting topics and projects.
Dynamic teacher.
